Invention: Voltaic Pile
Year actually invented: 1800
Earliest plausible: Flag
Earliest straightforward: Flag
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: Flag
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Flag
Geographic note: Flag
Explanation: Flagged. The voltaic pile is itself the foundational discovery of sustained current electricity and sits explicitly on the off-limits list. The binding constraint is not technological but conceptual-empirical: recognizing that stacking dissimilar-metal pairs separated by an electrolyte produces a continuous EMF. That recognition emerged from Galvani's 1791 frog-leg experiments and Volta's interpretive dispute — a chain of dedicated scientific investigation the engineer-artisan team cannot conduct.

possible_locations: England (London; Birmingham); France (Paris); Italy (Pavia); Germany (Berlin; Munich)
geographic_note: The actual location (London) closely matches possible locations. Any well-equipped European natural-philosophy lab with a large voltaic pile could have demonstrated the arc; Davy's priority reflects the Royal Institution's unusually large battery.
full_text:
Invention: Arc Lamp
Year actually invented: 1802
Earliest plausible: 1800–1805
Earliest straightforward: 1800–1810
Confidence: High
Actual location: Royal Institution, London, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England (London, Birmingham), France (Paris), Italy (Pavia), Germany (Berlin, Munich)
Geographic note: The actual location (London) closely matches the possible locations. Any well-equipped European natural-philosophy laboratory with access to a large voltaic pile could have demonstrated the arc; Davy's priority reflects the Royal Institution's unusually large battery rather than unique geographic conditions.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / large galvanic battery [1800] — external (off-limits phenomenon, must already exist)
- Charcoal or carbon rods [antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Simple adjustable electrode holders [antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Insulated wiring and conductors [late 18th c.] — external, non-binding
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Voltaic effect / sustained current [Volta 1800] — external; off-limits phenomenon
- Observation that current across a small gap between carbon electrodes produces a brilliant luminous arc — team-discoverable via direct iteration once a sufficiently large pile exists
Explanation: The arc lamp is essentially "connect a large enough voltaic pile to two carbon electrodes and draw them slightly apart." The binding item is the voltaic pile itself (1800). Once the pile is available, the arc effect emerges almost immediately from empirical iteration — Davy demonstrated it within ~two years of Volta's announcement, and any comparably equipped European lab assembling a large enough battery would have discovered the arc almost at once.

possible_locations: Germany (Nuremberg; Augsburg; Frankfurt); Italy (Venice; Padua; Bologna); France (Paris; Montpellier); Netherlands (Leiden; Amsterdam); Switzerland (Basel); England (London); Ottoman/Persian apothecary centers

The German origin reflects the strong apothecary-chemistry tradition rather than unique geographic necessity.
full_text:
Invention: Isolation of morphine
Year actually invented: 1804
Earliest plausible: 1500–1650
Earliest straightforward: 1750–1800
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Paderborn, Westphalia (Germany)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Germany (Nuremberg, Augsburg, Frankfurt), Italy (Venice, Padua, Bologna), France (Paris, Montpellier), Netherlands (Leiden, Amsterdam), Switzerland (Basel), England (London apothecaries' guild), Ottoman/Persian pharmacy traditions
Geographic note: The actual location (a small German apothecary shop) is one of many plausible sites — any well-equipped European apothecary or alchemical workshop with access to opium could have attempted this. The German origin reflects the strong apothecary-chemistry tradition of late-18th-century Germany rather than unique geographic necessity.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Crude opium [antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Glass distillation and extraction vessels [antiquity, refined Islamic alchemists ~9th c.] — external
- Ammonia / ammoniacal solutions (sal ammoniac) as alkaline precipitant [medieval Islamic alchemy] — external; binding
- Crystallization and recrystallization techniques [medieval alchemy] — external
- Distilled ethanol as extraction solvent [~12th c. Salerno] — external
- Filtration through cloth or paper [antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Analytical balance [available by 17th c.] — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical knowledge of acid-base reactions and salt formation [medieval/early-modern alchemy] — external
- Recognition that plant active principles can be concentrated by selective solvent extraction — external
- Concept of an isolable basic plant principle — team-discoverable via iteration on opium extracts
Explanation: Morphine isolation requires only practical bench techniques: extract opium with hot water or alcohol, treat with ammonia to precipitate the alkaline principle, and recrystallize. The binding item is the availability of an alkaline reagent (ammonia/sal ammoniac) plus crystallization technique, both in hand by the late medieval period. The 150–300 year gap reflects historical contingency — apothecaries treated opium as a whole-drug tincture and had no conceptual framework prompting them to seek a single crystalline basic principle. By the late 18th century, with Scheele's plant-acid isolations (1770s–80s), multiple apothecary-chemists across Europe were positioned to converge within a decade or two.

possible_locations: France (Lyon; Tours); Italy (Florence; Venice; Genoa; Milan); Flanders (Bruges; Brussels); Ottoman Bursa; Ming/Qing China (Suzhou; Nanjing)
geographic_note: The actual location (Lyon) was the dominant European silk-weaving center by 1800, but at the earliest plausible date Italian silk cities and Chinese drawloom centers were equally or more capable. The French origin reflects 18th-century patronage concentration and prior incremental work by Bouchon, Falcon, and Vaucanson, not a unique technical prerequisite.
full_text:
Invention: Jacquard Loom
Year actually invented: 1804
Earliest plausible: 1500–1600
Earliest straightforward: 1725–1750
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Lyon, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France (Lyon, Tours), Italy (Florence, Venice, Genoa, Milan), Flanders (Bruges, Brussels), Ottoman Bursa, Ming/Qing China (Suzhou, Nanjing)
Geographic note: The actual location (Lyon) was the dominant European silk-weaving center by 1800, but at the earliest plausible date Italian silk cities and Chinese drawloom centers were equally or more capable — China in particular had operated complex figure-weaving drawlooms for centuries. The French origin reflects 18th-century concentration of silk patronage and prior incremental work, not a unique technical prerequisite.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Drawloom for figured weaving [Han China ~1st c. BC; Europe by medieval period] — external (binding)
- Punched card or punched paper tape [Bouchon 1725; buildable from card stock since antiquity] — team-reachable
- Hooks, needles, springs in iron/brass [metalworking, antiquity onward] — external, non-binding
- Precision wire-drawing and small-scale springs [medieval Europe] — external, non-binding
- Card-lacing and chain mechanism to advance cards [team-buildable from clockwork tradition] — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Binary/discrete selection logic (hole vs. no hole) — team-discoverable via project iteration
- Mechanical principle that a row of independently selectable hooks can replace a human draw-boy — team-discoverable
Explanation: The Jacquard loom is essentially a drawloom with each warp-selection decision encoded on a punched card read by spring-loaded hooks. The binding item is the drawloom itself, securely available in Europe by the late medieval period and far earlier in China; everything else is straightforwardly buildable by a skilled clockmaker of 1500–1600. The 200–300 year gap reflects conceptual non-obviousness: weavers had a working human-powered solution (the draw-boy), so the incentive to mechanize selection emerged only with the 18th-century French silk industry's drive for productivity.

full_text:
Invention: High-pressure steam locomotive
Year actually invented: 1804
Earliest plausible: 1750–1770
Earliest straightforward: 1790–1800
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Pen-y-darren ironworks, Merthyr Tydfil, Wales, UK
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Britain (Cornwall, South Wales, Coalbrookdale, Newcastle/Tyneside, Birmingham), France (Paris, Le Creusot), Belgium (Liège), German Rhineland/Saxony, Sweden (Bergslagen)
Geographic note: The actual Welsh ironworks location aligns closely with the densest cluster of plausible locations — British coal-and-iron districts. Continental ironworks had broadly comparable capability by mid-18th century but lacked the same colliery tramway demand pull.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Cast-iron pressure vessel/boiler [feasible from late 17th c.; routine by mid-18th c.] — external, binding
- Precision boring of cylinders (Wilkinson's boring mill) [1774] — external; before this, leaky pistons cap usable pressure
- Cast-iron plateway / edge rails [in use by ~1760s] — external, non-binding
- Crank-and-flywheel rotative motion [antiquity; applied to steam by Pickard 1780] — external
- Slide/plug valves and eccentric-driven valve gear — team-reachable from Newcomen valve practice
- Wrought-iron frames, axles, wheels [long-established] — external, non-binding
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Atmospheric pressure and steam pressure as a usable force [Papin 1679; Savery 1698; Newcomen 1712] — external
- High-pressure steam exhausted to atmosphere yields more work per unit weight — team-discoverable via iteration
- Adhesion of smooth iron wheels on smooth iron rails sufficient for traction — team-discoverable empirically
Explanation: The binding item is reliable high-pressure boiler-and-cylinder construction; Papin's digester (1679) shows the principle was understood, but routinely tight cylinders awaited Wilkinson's 1774 boring mill, opening the straightforward window around 1790. An insightful team in a well-equipped ironworks of the 1750s–60s could plausibly have built a prototype. Watt's separate-condenser patent (1769) actively delayed high-pressure work in Britain until 1800; the ~50-year gap reflects patent suppression and a widespread fear that high-pressure boilers were uncontrollably dangerous.

possible_locations: China (Han dynasty medical centers); Japan (Kampo tradition; post-Tang transmission); India (Ayurvedic centers — Taxila; Kashi); Hellenistic/Roman Mediterranean (Alexandria; Rome); Persian/Islamic medical centers (Gondishapur; Baghdad; Cordoba)
geographic_note: The actual location (early 19th-century Japan) is much later than the earliest plausible window. Equivalent botanical pharmacopoeias existed in Han China, Ayurvedic India, and Greco-Roman medicine — any of which had the prerequisites for a working surgical anaesthetic preparation centuries earlier.
full_text:
Invention: General anaesthetic
Year actually invented: 1804
Earliest plausible: 200 BC–200 AD
Earliest straightforward: 1500–1700
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Japan (Hanaoka Seishu, Kii Province)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: China (Han dynasty medical centers), Japan (Kampo medical tradition, post Tang transmission), India (Ayurvedic centers such as Taxila, Kashi), Hellenistic/Roman Mediterranean (Alexandria, Rome), Persian/Islamic medical centers (Gondishapur, Baghdad, Cordoba)
Geographic note: The actual location (early 19th-century Japan) is much later than the earliest plausible window. Hanaoka's tsusensan was a refinement of an East Asian herbal tradition traceable to Hua Tuo's legendary mafeisan (~200 AD); equivalent botanical pharmacopoeias existed in Han China, Ayurvedic India, and Greco-Roman medicine, any of which had the prerequisites for a working surgical anaesthetic preparation.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Psychoactive/narcotic plants: Datura, opium poppy, mandrake, henbane, aconite, cannabis [all antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Decoction, infusion, and herbal compounding techniques [antiquity in all traditions] — external, non-binding
- Basic surgical instruments and practice [antiquity — Sushruta, Hippocratic corpus, Han China] — external, non-binding
- Oral or inhaled administration methods (sponges, drinks) [antiquity] — external, non-binding
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical knowledge that tropane alkaloids produce deep stupor and analgesia sufficient for surgery — team-discoverable through iterative dosing trials
- Knowledge that opium produces profound analgesia [Sumerian/Egyptian, 3rd–2nd millennium BC] — external
- Combination dosing to balance efficacy against lethality — team-discoverable through iteration
Explanation: A general anaesthetic requires only a reliable plant-derived sedative-analgesic compound dosed to render a patient unconscious long enough to operate. The binding item is empirical knowledge of tropane-alkaloid plants (Datura, mandrake, henbane), all known since antiquity, combined with opium. The ~1800-year gap to Hanaoka reflects: (a) the inherently narrow therapeutic window of these alkaloids making reliable dosing risky; (b) cultural/religious resistance to elective deep surgery suppressing demand; (c) loss of Hua Tuo's recipe and fragmentation of medical traditions. Hanaoka's achievement was careful empirical titration rather than any new prerequisite.

full_text:
Invention: Internal combustion engine "Pyreolophore"
Year actually invented: 1807
Earliest plausible: 1660–1690
Earliest straightforward: 1690–1750
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Chalon-sur-Saône, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, Netherlands, England, Italy, Germany
Geographic note: The earliest-plausible window aligns with the Royal Society/Académie des Sciences milieu where Huygens and Papin demonstrated gunpowder piston engines; France, the Netherlands, and England are all plausible venues, matching the actual French origin a century later.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Tight-fitting metal cylinder and piston with leather/hemp packing [c. 1650, Guericke vacuum pumps] — external
- Gunpowder/fine combustible powders (lycopodium known as flash powder) [pre-1500] — external
- Spring-loaded or weighted check valves [17th c. pumps] — external
- Flint/spark or slow-match ignition into a chamber [pre-1600] — external
- Fuel-metering bellows or powder-injection mechanism — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Combustion of confined powder produces a sudden large pressure pulse [pre-1500, cannon/petards] — external
- Air has weight / atmosphere can do work against a vacuum [Torricelli 1643, Guericke 1654] — external
- Cooling of hot combustion gases creates a partial vacuum usable to drive a piston [Huygens/Papin 1673–1680] — external; binding
- Lycopodium/fine-powder deflagration burns far faster than grain powder — team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding item is the closed-chamber combustion-piston insight, tied to the Torricelli–Guericke–Huygens–Papin lineage of the 1640s–1680s. Before atmospheric pressure was understood as a usable force, gunners treated confined powder only as a projectile launcher. Once Huygens/Papin (1673–1680) demonstrated the principle, a motivated team with lycopodium and improved valving could plausibly build a working Niépce-style engine within a few years, placing the earliest plausible window at 1660–1690.

full_text:
Invention: Commercial steamboat
Year actually invented: 1807
Earliest plausible: 1720–1740
Earliest straightforward: 1780–1800
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Hudson River, New York, USA (Fulton)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England (London, Birmingham, Newcastle), Scotland (Clyde), France (Paris, Lyon), Netherlands
Geographic note: The actual American location reflects commercial opportunity (long navigable rivers, scarce roads) more than unique technical capability; Britain and France had equal or superior engine-building capacity earlier.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Newcomen atmospheric engine [1712] — external (binding for early window)
- Watt separate-condenser engine [1769] — external (binding for straightforward window)
- Paddle wheel for propulsion [antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Wooden hull shipbuilding [antiquity] — external, non-binding
- Crank-and-connecting-rod for rotary motion [medieval] — external, non-binding
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Atmospheric pressure/vacuum (Torricelli, Pascal, 17th c.) — external
- Steam condensation produces usable vacuum work — team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding item is a steam engine light and powerful enough to drive a hull through water. A Newcomen-era engine (1712) could plausibly be adapted to a barge for slack-water river service by 1720–1740. The straightforward window opens once Watt's separate condenser (1769) yields a much better power-to-weight ratio — indeed Jouffroy (1783), Fitch (1787), and Symington (1788/1801) all built working steamboats in that window before Fulton's commercially successful North River Steamboat.

possible_locations: Roman Empire (Italy; Gaul); Han China; Hellenistic Egypt (Alexandria)
geographic_note: Actual invention occurred in Napoleonic France under explicit prize-driven institutional demand; France (as Gaul) is included among the plausible Roman-era locations, so the geographic origin is broadly aligned, though the specific mechanism differs.
full_text:
Invention: Canning process
Year actually invented: 1810
Earliest plausible: 100 BC–200 AD
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1750
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: France / Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Roman Empire (Italy, Gaul), Han China, Hellenistic Egypt (Alexandria)
Geographic note: Actual invention occurred in Napoleonic France under explicit prize-driven institutional demand; the earliest plausible locations (Roman/Han) had the requisite glassblowing, ceramics, metallurgy, and culinary culture but lacked the systematic empirical research program that would have motivated the work.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Hermetically sealable containers (glass bottles with cork/wax seal, or tin-lined soldered vessels, or glazed stoneware with wax/resin seal) [glassblowing ~50 BC; tinplate ~1200s but tin-lining of vessels possible in antiquity] — team-reachable (Roman glassblowers routinely produced thick-walled stoppered bottles; cork stoppers and wax/pitch sealing were standard for amphorae and unguent bottles)
- Sustained boiling-water bath / steam heating apparatus [available since antiquity] — external (bronze cauldrons, controlled hearths, and water baths (balneum) were standard in Roman kitchens and alchemical practice)
- Cork, wax, pitch, resin sealants [available since antiquity] — external (used routinely for amphora sealing in wine/oil/garum trade)
- Wire or cord bindings to hold stoppers against internal pressure [available] — external (basic metallurgy)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical observation that prolonged heating in a sealed container preserves food indefinitely [team-discoverable] — team-discoverable (Appert himself worked without germ theory; pure empirical iteration on time/temperature/seal-integrity suffices)
- No theory of microorganisms required [Pasteur 1860s irrelevant to invention] — external (Appert preceded germ theory by 50 years; the process is a pre-theoretical craft discovery)
- Recognition that air or "something in air" spoils food — team-discoverable (Roman writers already noted that excluded-air storage, e.g., oil-topped or wax-sealed preserves, lasted longer)
Explanation: The binding constraint is not technological but institutional-motivational: every physical input (thick glass bottles, corks, wax, cauldrons, sustained heat) existed in the Roman world, and Appert's own method required no scientific theory — he succeeded by stubborn empirical iteration. The reason no Roman, Chinese, Islamic, or medieval European artisan stumbled onto it across ~1900 years is threefold. First, antiquity and the medieval period had no concept of systematic empirical research into food preservation as a discrete technical problem; preservation was a household craft transmitted by tradition, not a target of organized experimentation by funded artisan-engineer teams. Second, an extensive and effective portfolio of alternatives — salting, smoking, drying, fermenting, pickling in vinegar or oil, sugar-preserving, ice-cellars, confit, and oil-capping — already solved the practical preservation needs of households, armies, and long-distance trade (garum, salt cod, hardtack, cured meats), so there was no economic pull toward a costly, breakable, fuel-intensive alternative. Third, the specific demand that finally produced canning was the unprecedented logistical pressure of mass conscript armies and blockaded navies in the Napoleonic era, which led the French Directory to offer a 12,000-franc prize in 1795 — an explicit institutional commission that funded Appert's fourteen years of iterative trials. Absent that prize-driven program, the combinatorial space (food type, fill level, seal method, boil duration, container shape) is large enough that no casual experimenter would traverse it. Appert's achievement was systematic iteration, not serendipity, so no flag is warranted; given a comparably motivated team and prize in antiquity, the process is reachable, but the "earliest straightforward" window only opens once early-modern state provisioning and scientific-society culture (~1700–1750) make such a directed program plausible.

possible_locations: Alexandria (Ptolemaic Egypt); Cos; Cnidus; Pergamon; Athens
geographic_note: Hellenistic medical centers (Alexandria, Cos, Cnidus, Pergamon, Athens) are the plausible earliest locations; Paris/France does not appear among them, representing a geographic mismatch explained by the Paris clinical school's unique 19th-century concentration of bedside pathological correlation.
full_text:
Invention: Stethoscope
Year actually invented: 1816
Earliest plausible: 300–100 BC
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1780 AD
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: France / Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Alexandria (Ptolemaic Egypt), Cos, Cnidus, Pergamon, Athens
Geographic note: Hellenistic medical centers (especially Alexandria under the Ptolemies) housed the only organized clinical traditions with anatomical investigation — Herophilus and Erasistratus were dissecting and timing pulses there. Republican Rome at this date lacked native medical infrastructure and imported Greek physicians only later, so it is excluded.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Turned/hollowed wooden tubes (woodworking lathe) [antiquity] — external/team-reachable
- Rolled parchment or papyrus cylinders [c. 2500 BC] — external/team-reachable
- Hippocratic immediate auscultation (ear-to-chest, succussion splash) [c. 400 BC] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Sound is conducted and can be channeled/amplified through solid and hollow media (everyday acoustics, e.g., speaking tubes) [antiquity] — external/team-discoverable
- Specific chest sounds correlate with specific internal conditions (rales, bronchophony, egophony) [team-discoverable empirically] — team-discoverable
- Clinical-pathological correlation: bedside signs map to post-mortem lesions [c. 1761, Morgagni; matured 1800s] — external for straightforward date; team-discoverable but heroic for earliest plausible
Explanation: The physical artifact — a hollow wooden cylinder — was trivially buildable in any Hellenistic workshop, and Hippocratic physicians already practiced immediate auscultation and succussion, so the binding constraint is conceptual, not material. A motivated Alexandrian team with access to Herophilan dissection, lab-scale clinical observation, and five years of iteration could plausibly notice that a rolled tube clarifies chest sounds and could begin cataloguing them, which is why 300–100 BC is reachable. The reason this did not happen historically for ~2000 years is that auscultation remained a marginal diagnostic compared to pulse lore, uroscopy, and humoral reasoning across Hippocratic, Galenic, Islamic, and medieval European medicine; without a systematic program of correlating bedside signs with internal lesions — a framework that only crystallized with Morgagni (1761), Bichat, and the Paris clinical school — there was no incentive structure to develop, refine, or transmit a sound-channeling instrument. The straightforward date of 1700–1780 reflects when post-Morgagni pathological anatomy and Auenbrugger's 1761 percussion made the conceptual scaffolding routine, leaving only the small mechanical step Laennec actually took.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Netherlands; Italy; German states; British North American colonies
geographic_note: Matches well — 18th-century European centers of electrical experimentation (Royal Society London, Paris, Leyden) had the artisan glass-blowing, instrument-making, and electrostatic know-how. Ronalds' 1816 London demonstration sits squarely in this milieu.
full_text:
Invention: Electric telegraph
Year actually invented: 1816
Earliest plausible: 1750–1770
Earliest straightforward: 1800–1820
Confidence: High
Actual location: London, United Kingdom
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Netherlands, Italy, German states, British North American colonies
Geographic note: Matches well — 18th-century European centers of electrical experimentation (Royal Society London, Paris, Leyden) had the artisan glass-blowing, instrument-making, and electrostatic know-how. Ronalds' 1816 London demonstration sits squarely in this milieu.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Frictional electrostatic generator [Hauksbee 1706, improved by Ramsden 1768] — external (mature by mid-18th c.)
- Leyden jar (charge storage) [1745–1746] — external (binding precursor)
- Insulated copper wire, silk/glass insulators [pre-1750] — external (artisan workshop standard)
- Pith-ball / gold-leaf electroscope as receiver [Canton 1754; Bennet 1787] — team-reachable (one layer)
- Synchronized rotating dials or alphabet selector (clockwork) [17th–18th c. horology] — team-reachable
- Long-span insulated overhead/buried line — team-reachable empirically
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Electrostatic charge transmits along conductors over long distances [Gray 1729; Du Fay 1733] — external (binding observation)
- Stephen Gray's ~800-ft signal transmission demonstration [1729] — external
- Two-fluid / one-fluid theory of electricity [Du Fay 1733; Franklin 1747] — external (sufficient working framework)
- Lesage's 24-wire alphabet telegraph proposal [1774] — external (proof-of-concept already published)
Explanation: The binding item is the Leyden jar (1745–46), which gave experimenters a storable, releasable charge sufficient to send a detectable pulse down a long insulated wire; Gray's 1729 demonstration of conduction over hundreds of feet had already shown the channel works. Once both existed, an electrostatic telegraph required only artisan-level integration: a frictional machine, an insulated line, an electroscope or pith-ball indicator, and an agreed encoding (single-wire timed dial, as Ronalds used, or Lesage's multi-wire alphabet). A motivated inventor with a five-year horizon could have done this any time after roughly 1750, and Lesage actually proposed it in 1774 — so the ~65-year gap to Ronalds reflects lack of perceived demand and the fragility/weather-sensitivity of electrostatic signaling, not missing technology. The galvanic approach (Sömmerring 1809) is a separate, post-1800 path and not required for the earliest plausible date.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; Netherlands; France; German states; Sweden
geographic_note: Skilled metalworking workshops capable of building tight-tolerance pistons and cylinders existed in several northern European centers by 1700; the actual Scottish origin reflects the later Industrial Revolution context, but the engine could plausibly have arisen wherever competent instrument-makers and millwrights worked.
full_text:
Invention: Stirling hot air engine
Year actually invented: 1816
Earliest plausible: 1700–1720
Earliest straightforward: 1760–1780
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Scotland, United Kingdom (Edinburgh/Kilmarnock)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, Netherlands, France, German states, Sweden
Geographic note: Skilled metalworking workshops capable of building tight-tolerance pistons and cylinders existed in several northern European centers by 1700; the actual Scottish origin reflects the later Industrial Revolution context, but the engine could plausibly have arisen wherever competent instrument-makers and millwrights worked.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Reasonably gas-tight piston-and-cylinder assembly [c. 1690, Papin/Huygens] — external (atmospheric engine experiments demonstrated workable cylinders)
- Crankshaft / flywheel mechanisms [ancient–medieval] — external (standard millwright practice)
- Brazed or riveted sheet-metal vessels for hot working chambers [17th c.] — external (coppersmiths routinely built pressure-tolerant vessels)
- Fine wire mesh or thin metal plates for the regenerator matrix [17th c., drawn wire industry] — external (drawn wire and rolled sheet available)
- Linkages for displacer + power piston (e.g., rhombic or simple beam) [pre-1700] — team-reachable (one layer of mechanical design)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Air expands when heated, contracts when cooled (Hero, Drebbel, Amontons ~1700) — external (well known qualitatively)
- Heat can be stored and released by a thermal mass (regenerator concept) — team-discoverable (an empirically reachable insight from blacksmithing/glassmaking practice; no caloric/thermodynamic theory needed)
- Closed-cycle operation distinguishable from atmospheric engines — team-discoverable (engineering insight, not new science)
Explanation: The Stirling engine needs no off-limits phenomenon: it relies on thermal expansion of air, a phenomenon understood qualitatively since antiquity and quantitatively by Amontons around 1700. The binding constraint is metalworking precision — specifically a reasonably gas-tight, thermally cycled piston/cylinder pair plus a fine regenerator matrix of wire gauze or thin plates. Both became routinely achievable in instrument-maker workshops by the late 17th century, which sets the earliest plausible window around 1700–1720, contemporaneous with Papin and Newcomen. By 1760–1780, with rolled sheet, drawn wire, and Wilkinson-grade boring on the horizon, a motivated inventor's workshop could straightforwardly produce a working low-pressure regenerative hot-air engine; the ~100-year gap to 1816 reflects the dominance of steam research and the absence of a champion focused on closed-cycle air rather than the historically contingent path of steam.

possible_locations: Northern Italy (Florence; Milan; Venice); Southern Germany (Nuremberg; Augsburg); Flanders (Bruges); France (Paris); Ottoman cities
geographic_note: Mannheim/Karlsruhe in 1817 fits the pattern of skilled Central European wheelwright and carriage-making centers; the earlier plausible locations are similar craft hubs (Italian Renaissance workshops, South German metalworking towns) with comparable wheelwright and joinery expertise.
full_text:
Invention: Dandy horse (Laufmaschine / velocipede)
Year actually invented: 1817
Earliest plausible: 1400–1500
Earliest straightforward: 1600–1700
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Germany / Mannheim, Germany
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Northern Italy (Florence, Milan, Venice), Southern Germany (Nuremberg, Augsburg), Flanders (Bruges), France (Paris), Ottoman cities
Geographic note: Mannheim/Karlsruhe in 1817 fits the pattern of skilled Central European wheelwright and carriage-making centers; the earlier plausible locations are similar craft hubs (Italian Renaissance workshops, South German metalworking towns) with comparable wheelwright and joinery expertise.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Spoked wooden wheels with iron tires [ancient, well established by 1000 CE] — external
- Wheelwright/carriage-making craft (axles, bearings, frames) [ancient; mature medieval guilds] — external
- Pivoting front axle / steerable carriage front [in use on wagons by 1300s] — external
- Forged iron fittings, pivot pins, bushings [medieval blacksmithing] — external
- Leather saddlery [ancient] — external
- Reasonably smooth roads or hard-packed paths [Roman roads onward; better in 18th–19th c.] — external (affects practicality, not feasibility)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Dynamic balance of a single-track vehicle (rider stabilizes by steering into a lean) [empirically discoverable in days of trial] — team-discoverable
- Castor/trail effect from a raked steering axis providing self-stabilization [empirically tunable] — team-discoverable
- No formal mechanics or Newtonian theory required; purely empirical — team-discoverable
Explanation: The dandy horse requires no scientific framework beyond what a competent wheelwright already knows; every component (spoked wheels, wooden frame, pivoting steerable fork, padded saddle) was within reach of medieval and Renaissance craft. The only non-obvious element is the counterintuitive insight that a two-wheeled vehicle is dynamically stable when moving and steered — but a five-year team iterating empirically would discover this within weeks of prototyping, as Drais himself effectively did. The binding constraint is therefore conceptual/cultural rather than technical: the idea of personal human-powered transport on two in-line wheels simply was not on anyone's agenda in eras dominated by horses, walking, and four-wheeled carts. Given the conceptual seed is granted by the rubric, a Renaissance Italian or 15th-century South German workshop (Leonardo-era craftsmanship) could have built and refined a working Laufmaschine; a 17th-century carriage shop could have done so trivially. The ~300-year historical gap is explained by absence of demand, cultural norms around dignified locomotion, and poor road surfaces outside cities, not by missing technology.

possible_locations: England; France; Netherlands; Italian states; German states; Ottoman Empire; Ming/Qing China; Mughal India
geographic_note: Requires a society with active large-scale civil engineering or mining culture, skilled iron/timber craftsmen, and an economic incentive (river crossing, deep mine, fortification sap). Renaissance Italy, early modern Netherlands, Tudor-Stuart England, and Ming China all qualify; England is included, matching the actual location.
full_text:
Invention: Tunneling shield
Year actually invented: 1818
Earliest plausible: 1500–1600
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1800
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: England / Rotherhithe, London, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Netherlands, Italian states, German states, Ottoman Empire, Ming/Qing China, Mughal India
Geographic note: Requires a society with active large-scale civil engineering or mining culture, skilled iron/timber craftsmen, and an economic incentive (river crossing, deep mine, fortification sap). Renaissance Italy, early modern Netherlands (drainage/canal works), Tudor-Stuart England, and Ming China all qualify; the inventor need not be a naturalist since the bivalve analogy is inspirational, not necessary.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Wrought iron plate and forged framing in large sections [pre-1500] — external
- Brickwork and hydraulic mortar/lime cement [Roman, widely available pre-1500] — external
- Heavy timber framing and screw jacks/wedges [pre-1500] — external
- Hand-powered pumps for dewatering (chain pump, Archimedes screw) [Antiquity] — external
- Mining shoring/cribbing and sapping techniques [medieval mining, pre-1500] — external
- Cast-iron components (optional, improves durability) [14th c. Europe; earlier in China] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical understanding of soft-ground earth pressure and the need for continuous face support [team-discoverable through iteration]
- Observation that small advancing cells with immediate lining prevent collapse (analogous to mining timbering or shipworm tubes) [team-discoverable]
- No modern soil mechanics or electromagnetism required
Explanation: The tunneling shield is a clever rearrangement of pre-existing civil and mining technology rather than a scientific breakthrough; nothing in it depends on any off-limits phenomenon. The binding constraint is large, reliable wrought-iron plate and framing capable of resisting soft-ground earth pressure across a multi-meter face, together with the social/economic motivation to attempt a soft-ground subaqueous or deep urban tunnel. Iron, brick, lime mortar, hand pumps, and timber shoring were all mature by 1500, so a motivated engineer-patron in Renaissance Italy, Elizabethan England, or Ming China could in principle have built a workable shield (1500–1600 plausible). However, before the canal age and industrial demand for under-river crossings, almost no one needed one, and pre-1700 iron production made multi-ton frames expensive and inconsistent; thus the straightforward window opens in the 1700s when British/Dutch ironworks and canal-driven tunneling demand converge, and Brunel's 1818 patent is essentially on time relative to that capability curve.

possible_locations: England; France; Netherlands; Switzerland; Germany (Saxony/Augsburg)
geographic_note: Requires a mature precision-clockmaking and instrument-making tradition; London, Paris, Geneva, and southern German clockmaking centers all qualify by the early 18th century. England's emerging industrial machine-tool culture gives it a slight edge for larger gear trains.
full_text:
Invention: Difference engine
Year actually invented: 1822
Earliest plausible: 1700–1750
Earliest straightforward: 1780–1820
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: England / London, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Netherlands, Switzerland, Germany (Saxony/Augsburg)
Geographic note: Requires a mature precision-clockmaking and instrument-making tradition; London, Paris, Geneva, and southern German clockmaking centers all qualify by the early 18th century. England's emerging industrial machine-tool culture gives it a slight edge for larger gear trains.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Precision gear cutting and clockmaker's lathes [c. 1670–1700] — external/team-reachable
- Screw-cutting and dividing engines (Ramsden 1770s) [1770s] — external/team-reachable; earlier hand-divided gears workable as one-layer precursor
- Cast iron and brass stock for frames and wheels [pre-1700] — external/team-reachable
- Stepped drum / pinwheel calculating mechanisms (Leibniz 1672, Pascal 1642) [1642–1672] — external/team-reachable
- Carry-propagation mechanisms in mechanical calculators [1642–1709] — external/team-reachable
- Stereotype/printing plate casting for output [c. 1700] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Method of finite differences for polynomial approximation (Newton's forward differences) [1687–1711] — external/team-discoverable
- Use of polynomials to approximate logarithmic, trigonometric, and navigational tables [late 1600s] — external/team-discoverable
- Recognition that human computer error in tables is the binding problem motivating mechanization [late 1600s onward] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint is precision metalworking at scale — the engine needs hundreds of near-identical gears, cams, and figure wheels with tight tolerances and reliable carry mechanisms, which only becomes routinely achievable with the late-17th-century maturation of clockmaker's lathes and dividing techniques (one allowed precursor layer covers Ramsden-style dividing engines if not yet extant). All conceptual prerequisites — Newton's finite differences, Pascal/Leibniz-style adders with carry, and the recognized need for error-free navigational and logarithmic tables — are in place by roughly 1710, so a motivated engineer-inventor with a top clockmaker's workshop (e.g., a Tompion or Graham equivalent) and five years could plausibly build a small-scale finite-difference machine by 1700–1750. The "straightforward" window of 1780–1820 reflects when Maudslay/Bramah-era machine tools make the mechanical reproducibility nearly routine, essentially bracketing Babbage's actual date.

possible_locations: Nuremberg (Germany); Augsburg (Germany); Florence/Milan (Italy); Paris (France); Antwerp (Spanish Netherlands); London (England)
geographic_note: The earliest plausible window centers on Renaissance European workshops where ornamental turning, clockwork, and armorers' template-copying converged; this is geographically distinct from the actual 1822 Massachusetts armory context but mechanically continuous with the same craft tradition.
full_text:
Invention: Pattern-tracing lathe (Blanchard lathe)
Year actually invented: 1822
Earliest plausible: 1500–1600
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1780
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: United States / Springfield Armory, Massachusetts, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Nuremberg (Germany), Augsburg (Germany), Florence/Milan (Italy), Paris (France), Antwerp (Spanish Netherlands), London (England)
Geographic note: The earliest plausible window centers on Renaissance European workshops where ornamental turning, clockwork, and armorers' template-copying converged; this is geographically distinct from the actual 1822 Massachusetts armory context but mechanically continuous with the same craft tradition.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Foot/pole-treadle lathe with rigid bed and tool rest [pre-1500; widespread in European turners' shops by 1480s] — external (standard turner's equipment, mature centuries before)
- Cam-and-follower mechanism (wheel/roller riding a shaped profile) [c. 1300s–1400s; used in trip-hammers, fulling mills, cam-driven automata, and the camshafts described by Villard de Honnecourt c. 1230 and elaborated by Taccola and Francesco di Giorgio in the 1400s] — external (this, not the pantograph, is the binding kinematic principle of the Blanchard lathe; a follower wheel tracing a wooden master is identical in principle to a cam follower tracing a cam profile)
- Template/profile copying in armorers' and stockmakers' shops [1500s; gunstock "patterns" and armor templates were standard shop practice] — external (motivates and demonstrates the master-pattern paradigm)
- Rose-engine / ornamental turning with rosette-and-rubber (a wheel-follower riding a shaped wooden/metal master to modulate cutter position) [documented by mid-1500s in southern German and Italian princely workshops; Plumier 1701 codifies prior practice] — external/team-reachable (this is mechanically a cam-follower copying lathe; it directly provides the wheel-on-master geometry Blanchard used)
- Hardened steel cutters and abrasive/cutter-wheel shaping [pre-1500; bladesmiths and millwrights] — external
- Spring or weight loading to keep follower in contact with master [pre-1500; crossbow springs, clock mainsprings c. 1400s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Geometric principle that a follower constrained to a master profile transfers that profile to a synchronized cutter [empirical, demonstrable since antiquity in copying and tracing crafts] — team-discoverable (no theory required; pure kinematic intuition available to any millwright)
- Recognition that an irregular (non-axisymmetric) solid can be generated by varying tool-to-axis distance as a function of rotation angle [empirical; implicit in oval-turning lathes documented by Leonardo's circle and built in 16th-c. ornamental turning] — team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding prerequisite is the cam-follower mechanism with a wheel riding a shaped master, NOT Scheiner's 1603 pantograph; the Blanchard lathe is a cam-follower copier, not a proportional linkage copier. Cam-and-follower kinematics were fully mature by the 1400s (trip-hammers, automata, Francesco di Giorgio's machine drawings), and by the mid-1500s southern German and Italian princely workshops were already building rose-engine/ornamental lathes in which a hardened wheel rode a shaped wooden rosette to modulate a cutter's position against a rotating workpiece — the exact geometry Blanchard used, only with a gunstock-shaped master instead of a decorative rosette. A motivated 16th-century team in a Nuremberg or Florentine arms-and-clockwork shop, given the gunstock-copying problem, could plausibly enlarge that ornamental-turning principle to a longitudinally-traversing carriage carrying both follower-wheel and cutter-wheel against a full-length wooden master; the one-layer precursor build is the traversing carriage with synchronized follower and cutter, on top of extant rose-engine kinematics. The straightforward window slips to 1700–1780 because by then Plumier's 1701 treatise had codified the technique and 18th-century French and English armory practice routinely used template gauges, making the conceptual leap nearly trivial for any well-equipped armorer.

possible_locations: France; Britain; German states; Netherlands; Italy
geographic_note: France matches well — late-18th-century French engraving and lithography circles were the natural milieu, and Britain (Wedgwood/Davy) was the only other place with comparable practitioner overlap between chemistry and image-making.
full_text:
Invention: Heliography
Year actually invented: 1822
Earliest plausible: 1780–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1800–1820
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: France / Saint-Loup-de-Varennes, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, Britain, German states, Netherlands, Italy
Geographic note: France matches well — late-18th-century French engraving and lithography circles were the natural milieu, and Britain (Wedgwood/Davy) was the only other place with comparable practitioner overlap between chemistry and image-making.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Polished pewter/silver/glass plates [pre-1700] — external (standard engraver/mirror-maker stock)
- Camera obscura with ground-glass and achromatic lens [c. 1758, Dollond] — external (widely used by artists by mid-1700s)
- Bitumen of Judea, refined and dissolved in oil of lavender/turpentine [pre-1700 as varnish; photoresist use documented late 1700s] — external (engravers' varnish tradition)
- Acid etching of metal plates (aquatint/etching) [pre-1700, refined 1760s] — external (printmaker craft)
- Oil of lavender / petroleum solvents as differential developer [pre-1700] — external (perfumery and varnish trade)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Light darkens silver salts (Schulze) [1727] — external (published, widely repeated)
- Light hardens/insolubilizes bitumen of Judea [first documented use by engravers late 1700s; explicitly published by Niépce c. 1822] — FLAG: discovery is plausibly serendipitous; not derivable from theory
- Differential solubility of exposed vs. unexposed bitumen in lavender oil [Niépce, c. 1822] — team-discoverable ONLY ONCE photosensitivity is known (routine solvent screening)
- Geometric/optical projection through camera obscura [pre-1700] — external
Explanation: The binding item is the photosensitivity of bitumen of Judea, and it deserves a serendipity flag. There is no documented pre-Niépce publication of bitumen photohardening that a 1700–1750 team could exploit; engravers handled bitumen as a varnish and acid-resist for a century without noticing the effect, and the earliest practitioner awareness only surfaces in the late 1700s. A "screen every common substance for light-induced change" program is in principle within engineer-style empirical iteration, but bitumen's effect is slow, subtle, and only legible after a specific solvent (lavender oil) is applied to wash away unhardened material — a two-variable coincidence that real practitioners missed for generations, making the discovery directable in hindsight but genuinely accidental in practice. Therefore the earliest straightforward date is 1800–1820, once bitumen photoresist behavior was circulating among engravers and a directed team could combine it with the camera obscura and Schulze's silver-salt knowledge; 1780–1800 is the earliest plausible only by granting an aggressive but not impossible photoresist-screening program in a French/British engraving workshop, and even that is flagged.

possible_locations: Germany; England; France; Sweden; Netherlands
geographic_note: Requires access to platinum (a Spanish/South American import refined in Europe from the mid-18th century) and a chemical-pneumatic workshop tradition; such conditions existed in major European centers of pneumatic chemistry (London, Paris, Stockholm, German university towns).
full_text:
Invention: First lighter (Döbereiner's Lamp)
Year actually invented: 1823
Earliest plausible: 1790–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1815–1825
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: Jena, Germany (Grand Duchy of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Germany, England, France, Sweden, Netherlands
Geographic note: Requires access to platinum (a Spanish/South American import refined in Europe from the mid-18th century) and a chemical-pneumatic workshop tradition; such conditions existed in major European centers of pneumatic chemistry (London, Paris, Stockholm, German university towns).
Prerequisite technologies:
- Hydrogen gas production (zinc/iron + dilute sulfuric acid) [1766, Cavendish] — team-reachable
- Malleable/spongy platinum metallurgy (Chabaneau, Wollaston processes) [1786–1805] — external
- Glassblown gas apparatus, stopcocks, pneumatic trough [c. 1770] — team-reachable
- Dilute mineral acids (sulfuric acid via lead chamber) [1746] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Composition of water / hydrogen as a distinct flammable gas [1783–1785, Lavoisier/Cavendish] — external
- Observation that finely divided platinum glows/ignites in hydrogen-air mixtures [1817, Davy/Döbereiner] — team-discoverable but serendipitous
- Concept of catalysis as a named phenomenon [1835, Berzelius] — not required (empirical use suffices)
Explanation: All material prerequisites — hydrogen generation from acid-on-metal, glass gas apparatus, and workable platinum sponge — were available by roughly 1790–1805, so a workshop could in principle assemble the device once it knew to direct H2 onto Pt sponge. The binding constraint is the empirical observation itself: the catalytic ignition of hydrogen on platinum is a serendipitous discovery (Davy's 1817 platinum-wire glow led directly to Döbereiner's 1823 lamp), with no theoretical framework predicting it before the 19th century. A motivated team with platinum sponge and hydrogen could plausibly stumble onto the effect during systematic combustion experiments after malleable platinum became available (~1790s), giving an earliest plausible window of 1790–1810; however, because the core phenomenon is accidental rather than deducible, this is FLAGGED. The straightforward window (1815–1825) reflects the actual path once Davy's observation was in hand.

possible_locations: France; Prussia; Saxony; Habsburg Austria; England; Dutch Republic; Sweden; Northern Italy
geographic_note: Requires a mature gunsmithing tradition with skilled artisans capable of precision metal fitting and a culture of military firearms innovation; major European gunmaking centers (Liège, Suhl, Saint-Étienne, London, Brescia) all qualify.
full_text:
Invention: Bolt-action rifle
Year actually invented: 1824
Earliest plausible: 1700–1730
Earliest straightforward: 1750–1780
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Sömmerda, Prussia (modern Germany)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, Prussia, Saxony, Habsburg Austria, England, Dutch Republic, Sweden, Northern Italy
Geographic note: Requires a mature gunsmithing tradition with skilled artisans capable of precision metal fitting and a culture of military firearms innovation; major European gunmaking centers (Liège, Suhl, Saint-Étienne, London, Brescia) all qualify.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Rifled barrels [c. 1500] — external
- Flintlock / fulminate priming compounds (mercury fulminate isolated 1800; rough percussion known earlier via fulminating gold/silver from 1600s) [1600s–1800] — team-reachable for primer chemistry
- Precision screw-cutting and metal lathe work [1700s] — external
- Paper cartridges (already standard military issue) [c. 1580–1600] — external
- Breech-loading firearm concepts (e.g., Ferguson rifle 1776; earlier Italian/German breechloaders 1500s) [1500s] — external
- Spring-loaded firing mechanisms (clockwork, wheel-lock springs) [1500s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical knowledge of obturation (sealing the breech against gas leakage) [empirical, team-discoverable through iteration]
- Empirical chemistry of impact-sensitive primers (fulminating compounds known from alchemical tradition) [1600s] — team-discoverable
Explanation: The bolt-action breech-loading needle gun is fundamentally a clever mechanical assembly of components that all existed by the early 1700s: rifled barrels, paper cartridges, spring-driven strikers, and precision-machined sliding/locking metal parts within the skill of clockmakers and master gunsmiths. The binding constraint is a reliable impact-sensitive primer compound: mercury fulminate was not isolated until 1800, but fulminating gold and silver were known from the 17th century and could plausibly be developed empirically into a workable priming compound by a skilled artisan team within five years. A motivated team in c. 1700–1730 working at Liège, Suhl, or Saint-Étienne could iterate on a sliding-bolt breech with a needle striker piercing a paper cartridge to hit a fulminate primer at the bullet base. The "straightforward" window slides to 1750–1780, when screw-cutting, spring steel, and chemical knowledge of fulminates were all more mature, making the design less speculative and closer to what Ferguson and others were already attempting.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Denmark; German states (Prussia; Bavaria); Italian states; Netherlands; United States (northeast)
geographic_note: Any well-equipped natural philosophy workshop in early-19th-century Europe with access to voltaic piles and insulated wire could replicate the work; Copenhagen, Paris, London, and Munich were all active centers within months of Oersted's announcement.
full_text:
Invention: Electromagnet
Year actually invented: 1824
Earliest plausible: 1820–1822
Earliest straightforward: 1822–1825
Confidence: High
Actual location: United Kingdom / Woolwich, United Kingdom
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Denmark, German states (Prussia, Bavaria), Italian states, Netherlands, United States (northeast)
Geographic note: Any well-equipped natural philosophy workshop in early-19th-century Europe with access to voltaic piles and insulated wire could replicate the work; Copenhagen, Paris, London, and Munich were all active centers within months of Oersted's announcement.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / galvanic battery [1800] — external (off-limits phenomenon, Volta)
- Soft iron of reasonable purity (wrought iron, horseshoe-forgeable) [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Insulated copper wire (silk-wrapped or shellac/varnish-coated) [c. 1820] — team-reachable (trivial artisan task: winding wire and lacquering or wrapping with silk)
- Compass / galvanometer for detecting current and field [pre-1800; Schweigger multiplier 1820] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Oersted's effect: electric current deflects a compass needle [1820] — external (off-limits, cannot be team-discovered)
- Ampere's solenoid/coil observations (current loops act as magnets) [1820] — external, but team-reachable as one-layer empirical extension of Oersted
- Arago's observation that a current-carrying wire magnetizes iron filings / steel needles [1820] — external/team-reachable
Explanation: The binding constraint is Oersted's 1820 discovery that current deflects a compass — this is explicitly off-limits as a team discovery, and without it there is no reason to wind a current-carrying wire around iron. Once Oersted's result is published (July–September 1820), the remaining steps are straightforward empirical engineering well within a skilled artisan workshop's five-year window: Arago and Ampere already showed within months of Oersted that a coiled wire magnetizes iron, so wrapping insulated wire around a soft-iron core is a one-layer extension — not a new scientific framework. Insulated wire (silk-wrapped or varnished) and soft wrought iron were both routine artisan products. Therefore the earliest plausible date is essentially 1820–1822 (immediately after Oersted, allowing months for the team to obtain the news, build piles, and iterate on core geometry and insulation), and the earliest straightforward date is 1822–1825, matching Sturgeon's actual 1824 demonstration. Confidence is High because the historical record shows multiple workers converging on this result within four years of Oersted with no missing intermediate technology.

possible_locations: England; France; Germany (Prussia/Saxony); Sweden; Netherlands
geographic_note: Requires a region with active pneumatic/oxidizer chemistry (post-Lavoisier/Berthollet) plus skilled apothecary-pyrotechnic trades; Paris, London, and major German university towns all qualify by the 1790s.
full_text:
Invention: Friction match
Year actually invented: 1826
Earliest plausible: 1790–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1800–1815
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: England / Stockton-on-Tees, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Germany (Prussia/Saxony), Sweden, Netherlands
Geographic note: Requires a region with active pneumatic/oxidizer chemistry (post-Lavoisier/Berthollet) plus skilled apothecary-pyrotechnic trades; Paris, London, and major German university towns all qualify by the 1790s.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Antimony sulfide (stibnite) [antiquity] — external
- Sulfur, refined [antiquity] — external
- Gum arabic / starch binder [antiquity] — external
- Wooden splints, sandpaper / glass-paper [18th c., glass-paper patented 1834 but coarse abrasive papers earlier] — external
- Phosphorus (alternative oxidant pathway) [1669, Brand] — external
- Small-batch crystallization / wet chemistry glassware [18th c.] — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Synthesis of potassium chlorate (KClO3) [Berthollet, 1786] — external
- Knowledge that chlorate + combustible mixtures detonate/ignite under friction or impact [Berthollet's chlorate gunpowder trials, 1788, with the Essonne explosion making the hazard widely known] — external
- Empirical observation that mechanical friction can initiate exothermic decomposition of sensitive oxidizers [late 18th c. pyrotechnic practice] — team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding item is potassium chlorate (Berthollet, 1786); every prior ignition chemistry (sulfur tips, phosphorus tapers, Chancel's 1805 chlorate-dip-in-acid match) lacked a friction-only initiation, and friction sensitivity is precisely the property chlorate-plus-sulfide mixtures provide. Once Berthollet's chlorate was reproducible (late 1780s) and its dangerous friction/impact sensitivity was a known nuisance to pyrotechnicians (by ~1788), a motivated team could empirically iterate splint coatings of KClO3 + Sb2S3 + binder against an abrasive strip within a few seasons; antimony sulfide is ancient, binders are trivial, and abrasive papers existed. FLAG: Walker's own discovery is reported as accidental (scraping a dried globule on stone), but the underlying chemistry was directable rather than serendipitous — a team briefed on chlorate's friction sensitivity would deliberately test friction ignition, so the accident is not load-bearing. The ~25-year gap to 1826 reflects (a) post-Essonne fear of chlorate causing chemists to avoid friction-sensitive formulations, (b) Napoleonic-era disruption of French chemical industry, and (c) commercial focus on the safer Chancel chemical match (1805) until Walker revisited friction initiation.

possible_locations: Liège region (Prince-Bishopric of Liège); Rhineland/Siegerland (Holy Roman Empire); Styria/Carinthia (Habsburg lands); Lombardy/Bergamo (Italy); Sweden (Bergslagen); Wealden England; Namur (Low Countries)
geographic_note: A blast-furnace culture using waterwheel-driven bellows had spread across a belt from the southern Low Countries through the Rhineland and the Alps by c.1400, with Sweden and England catching up shortly after. Any of these ironmaking regions had the masonry, bellows, and charcoal supply chains needed; the binding constraint is craft tradition rather than geography.
full_text:
Invention: Hot-blast iron smelting
Year actually invented: 1828
Earliest plausible: 1400–1500
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1750
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Scotland, UK (Glasgow)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Liège region (Prince-Bishopric of Liège), Rhineland/Siegerland (Holy Roman Empire), Styria/Carinthia (Habsburg lands), Lombardy/Bergamo (Italy), Sweden (Bergslagen), Wealden England, Namur (Low Countries)
Geographic note: A blast-furnace culture using waterwheel-driven bellows had spread across a belt from the southern Low Countries through the Rhineland and the Alps by c.1400, with Sweden and England catching up shortly after. Any of these ironmaking regions had the masonry, bellows, and charcoal supply chains needed; the binding constraint is craft tradition rather than geography.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Blast furnace with water-powered bellows [c.1150–1400] — external
- Refractory masonry / firebrick capable of sustained high temperatures [antiquity, refined medieval] — external
- Wrought-iron or cast-iron pipework able to carry hot air [available by 1400 in lab quantities] — team-reachable
- Auxiliary firebox / stove (a second small hearth to heat the blast pipe) [trivially constructible] — team-reachable
- Leather or wooden double-acting bellows producing steady draft [c.1300] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical observation that hotter tuyere air yields more iron per unit charcoal [team-discoverable by direct trial] — team-discoverable
- Observation that summer (warm-air) blasts performed worse than winter blasts — historically the misleading folk belief that had to be overturned [team-discoverable] — team-discoverable
- No new scientific framework required; thermodynamics and combustion chemistry are NOT prerequisites (Neilson himself worked without them)
Explanation: Hot-blast is conceptually a one-layer add-on to the existing blast furnace: route the tuyere air through an iron pipe passing over a small auxiliary fire before it enters the hearth. Every physical ingredient — masonry furnaces, water-powered bellows, iron piping, and auxiliary fireboxes — was in routine use across European ironmaking regions by the 15th century, and lab-quantity wrought- or cast-iron tubing was within reach of any competent bloomery/blast-furnace workshop. The binding constraint is therefore not materials or theory but the entrenched empirical belief, dominant well into the 1820s, that a cold blast was superior (winter campaigns outperformed summer ones, which ironmasters wrongly attributed to air temperature rather than humidity). A motivated team given five years and permission to iterate empirically could falsify this folk rule and build a working stove-and-pipe arrangement in any major 15th-century ironmaking center, making 1400–1500 plausible. The "straightforward" window of 1700–1750 reflects the era when coke smelting, larger furnaces, and improved iron tubing made the redesign almost an obvious efficiency tweak; the 78-year gap to Neilson is essentially institutional inertia, not a missing prerequisite.

possible_locations: Italy; France; Germany; Netherlands; England; Ottoman Empire; Ming China
geographic_note: Requires a region with skilled metalworking artisans (clockmakers, gunsmiths, millwrights), draft horses or oxen, and broad-acre cereal cultivation — conditions met across Renaissance Europe and East Asia.
full_text:
Invention: Reaping machine
Year actually invented: 1828
Earliest plausible: 1500–1600
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1800
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Carmyllie, Scotland, United Kingdom
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Italy, France, Germany, Netherlands, England, Ottoman Empire, Ming China
Geographic note: Requires a region with skilled metalworking artisans (clockmakers, gunsmiths, millwrights), draft horses or oxen, and broad-acre cereal cultivation—conditions met across Renaissance Europe and East Asia.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Wrought iron / spring steel for blades and reciprocating linkages [c. 1400] — external
- Crank-and-connecting-rod and gear trains (mill technology, clockwork) [c. 1300] — external
- Wheeled wagon / cart with strong axle for ground-drive power takeoff [antiquity] — external
- Harness and draft animal traction (horse collar) [c. 900] — external
- Canvas/linen sailcloth for collection apron [antiquity] — external
- Forged shears / scissor mechanisms as cutting principle [antiquity] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical observation that reciprocating shear-cut severs stalks more cleanly than chopping [team-discoverable] — team-discoverable
- Recognition that ground-wheel rotation can be geared to drive a cutter bar [team-discoverable] — team-discoverable
- Roman vallus (Pliny, Palladius) demonstrated mechanical harvesting was possible [c. 70 CE] — external
Explanation: A reaping machine is a purely mechanical device whose every component—iron cutter blades, crank-driven reciprocation, gear trains, wheeled chassis, draft harness, canvas apron—existed by the late medieval period; the Roman vallus even proved animal-pushed harvesters were feasible in antiquity. The binding constraint is therefore not any prerequisite technology or scientific theory, but rather the empirical engineering integration of a scissor-action cutter bar geared to a ground wheel, with gathering fingers to present stalks to the blade. A motivated inventor with a skilled clockmaker/millwright workshop in Renaissance Italy or Northern Europe (c. 1500–1600) had every needed material and mechanism, making this the earliest plausible window; by the 18th century, with refined spring steel and precision linkages widely available to estate engineers, construction becomes straightforward, which matches the historical clustering of attempts (Boyce 1799, Gladstone 1806, Bell 1828) once agricultural-improvement incentives aligned.

possible_locations: England (Cornwall; Birmingham; London); Scotland (Glasgow); France (Paris)
geographic_note: Strong match. The earliest plausible window centers on British industrial regions where coal-gas experimentation was already underway; Murdoch's Redruth and Birmingham work and Lebon's Paris demonstrations bracket the realistic geography.
full_text:
Invention: Gas Stove
Year actually invented: 1826
Earliest plausible: 1792–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1815–1826
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Northampton, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England (Cornwall, Birmingham, London), Scotland (Glasgow), France (Paris)
Geographic note: Strong match. The earliest plausible window centers on British industrial regions where coal-gas experimentation was already underway; Murdoch's Redruth and Birmingham work and Lebon's Paris demonstrations bracket the realistic geography.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Coal retort / gas distillation apparatus [1792] — team-reachable (one-layer precursor: Murdoch's Redruth gasworks demonstrated controllable coal-gas production with extant metallurgy and glassblowing)
- Iron pipe and brass cock/valve fittings [pre-1700] — external (mature plumbing trades supplied gas-tight conduits and adjustable taps)
- Burner orifice / lamp jet machining [c. 1780] — external (Argand lamp era; precision drilling of small brass jets routine in workshops)
- Flint-and-steel or taper ignition [ancient] — external
- Cast-iron stove and trivet construction [18th c.] — external (Franklin/Rumford stove tradition)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Coal yields flammable gas on distillation [Clayton, 1684] — external (published observation)
- Gas burns with a luminous, hot, regulable flame proportional to flow [Murdoch/Lebon, 1792–1801] — team-discoverable (direct empirical observation)
- Air admixture sustains combustion — team-discoverable (empirical tuning of air-holes in the burner)
Explanation: The binding precursor is Murdoch's 1792 Redruth coal-gas demonstration, so the earliest plausible window opens at 1792 and not before. While Clayton's 1684 observation establishes the phenomenon, a five-year engineering team needs a working coal-gas generation and distribution scheme as its one allowed precursor layer; building that gasworks AND the stove from a bare 1684 observation would require chaining two precursor layers, which the rubric forbids. Once Murdoch's gasworks exists as an extant technology, piping gas to a perforated iron burner under a trivet is straightforward iron-and-brass workshop engineering — burners, cocks, and cast-iron stove bodies were all mature. The 26-year gap to Sharp's 1826 commercialization reflects market and infrastructure factors (municipal gas mains, public acceptance, competition with coal ranges) rather than missing technology, which is why the straightforward window opens around 1815 once urban gas mains made domestic supply practical.

possible_locations: United Kingdom (London); France (Paris); Denmark (Copenhagen); German states (Berlin; Munich); Habsburg Empire (Vienna; Pest); Italian states; United States (Philadelphia; Boston)
geographic_note: Once Oersted's 1820 announcement propagated through European scientific journals within months, any well-equipped workshop in a major European or American scientific center with access to voltaic piles, copper wire, and skilled instrument makers could attempt the construction. Centers with strong instrument-making traditions (London, Paris, Copenhagen, Vienna) had the greatest concentration of relevant artisans.
full_text:
Invention: Commutated electromagnetic motor
Year actually invented: 1828
Earliest plausible: 1821–1823
Earliest straightforward: 1825–1828
Confidence: High
Actual location: Hungary / Pest, Hungary (Habsburg Empire)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (London), France (Paris), Denmark (Copenhagen), German states (Berlin, Munich), Habsburg Empire (Vienna, Pest), Italian states, United States (Philadelphia, Boston)
Geographic note: Once Oersted's 1820 announcement propagated through European scientific journals within months, any well-equipped workshop in a major European or American scientific center with access to voltaic piles, copper wire, and skilled instrument makers could attempt the construction. Centers with strong instrument-making traditions (London, Paris, Copenhagen, Vienna) had the greatest concentration of relevant artisans.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / battery [1800] — external (off-limits phenomenon, but extant)
- Insulated copper wire (silk/shellac wrapped) [c. 1820] — team-reachable
- Soft iron cores and precision machining (lathes, bearings) [pre-1800] — external
- Mercury cup contacts / sliding brush contacts [c. 1820, Faraday's homopolar 1821] — team-reachable
- Mechanical commutator (segmented sliding contact) [team-buildable single layer] — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Oersted's effect: current-carrying wire deflects compass [1820] — external (off-limits)
- Ampère's solenoid/electromagnet behavior [1820] — external
- Empirical observation that reversing current reverses force direction [1820] — team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint is Oersted's 1820 discovery that electric current produces a magnetic field, which is explicitly off-limits as a team-discoverable phenomenon. Once this is published (and the voltaic pile already existed since 1800), the path to a commutated rotary motor is a straightforward empirical engineering problem: wind coils around iron cores to amplify the force, mount them on a shaft, and devise a sliding contact that reverses current at the right rotational position to sustain torque. Faraday demonstrated continuous rotation (homopolar) in 1821, and the conceptual leap to a segmented commutator is a single layer of mechanical precursor tech well within an artisan workshop's capability — segmented sliding contacts are a modest extension of existing rotary switching hardware. Therefore 1821–1823 is plausible immediately after Oersted's results circulated, with 1825–1828 being the straightforward window once electromagnet design (Sturgeon 1825) was widely known.

possible_locations: Roman Empire; Han China; Ptolemaic/Roman Egypt; Hellenistic Mediterranean
geographic_note: France did not exist in the earliest plausible period (100 BCE–200 CE); plausible locations are Mediterranean and East Asian literate civilizations of that era. The actual 1829 France location is a chronological mismatch rather than a surprising geographical one.
full_text:
Invention: Braille tactile alphabet
Year actually invented: 1829
Earliest plausible: 100 BCE – 200 CE
Earliest straightforward: 1500 – 1700 CE
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: France / Coupvray, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Roman Empire, Han China, Ptolemaic/Roman Egypt, Hellenistic Mediterranean
Geographic note: Any literate society with paper-like or parchment-like substrates and a culture supporting blind individuals (or scribes) could have developed this. Requires only a writing-literate culture and someone motivated to encode text tactilely.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Paper or parchment/vellum capable of holding embossed dots without tearing [c. 200 BCE papyrus; c. 100 CE paper in China; parchment c. 200 BCE] — external/team-reachable
- Pointed stylus / awl [Neolithic] — external/team-reachable
- Phonetic or syllabic alphabet (small symbol inventory) [c. 1050 BCE Phoenician; Greek c. 800 BCE] — external/team-reachable
- Night-writing / sonography precedent (Barbier's military code) — NOT required; Braille's innovation is conceptual, not technological [1815] — team-discoverable independently
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Tactile two-point discrimination on fingertip (~2-3 mm resolution) — empirically discoverable in days [ancient, observable] — team-discoverable
- Combinatorial insight: a 2x3 dot cell yields 63 distinct patterns, sufficient for an alphabet [combinatorics known to Greeks; trivially discoverable empirically] — team-discoverable
- Recognition that blind people can learn systematic codes [observable from any blind person learning speech] — team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint is conceptual, not material — every physical prerequisite (smooth durable sheet material, pointed stylus, phonetic alphabet with ~25 symbols) existed by roughly 100 BCE in multiple literate civilizations. A motivated team with a skilled artisan could empirically determine optimal dot spacing and cell size within weeks by testing on blind subjects, and the 2x3 grid emerges naturally from fingertip-pad geometry. The reason for the ~1900-year historical gap is sociological rather than technical: pre-modern societies lacked organized education for the blind, blind individuals were rarely literate stakeholders in writing systems, and cheap embossable paper for personal use only became widespread much later. The "earliest straightforward" range (1500-1700) reflects when rag paper was abundant and individual literacy plus humanist interest in disability pedagogy made the social conditions plausible (cf. Francisco Lucas's 1517 carved-wood letters for the blind, Harsdörffer's 1651 wax tablet proposal).

possible_locations: England; France; Netherlands; Germany (Saxony/Harz mining regions); Sweden
geographic_note: Regions with advanced metalworking, mining-pump traditions, and skilled cylinder-boring artisans are the natural homes; English and Continental mining districts already used force-pumps and air-engines for ventilation.
full_text:
Invention: Compound air compressor
Year actually invented: 1829
Earliest plausible: 1700–1730
Earliest straightforward: 1760–1790
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: United Kingdom
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Netherlands, Germany (Saxony/Harz mining regions), Sweden
Geographic note: Regions with advanced metalworking, mining-pump traditions, and skilled cylinder-boring artisans are the natural homes; English and Continental mining districts already used force-pumps and air-engines for ventilation.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Bored metal cylinders with reasonably tight pistons [c. 1700, refined by Smeaton/Wilkinson 1770s] — team-reachable
- Reciprocating force pumps and single-stage air pumps (Guericke/Hauksbee/Papin lineage) [1650–1700] — external
- Leather/hemp packing and gland seals for piston rods [1600s] — external
- Non-return (clack/poppet) valves from waterworks and mine pumps [1600s] — external
- Riveted/strapped wrought-iron or cast-bronze pressure vessels [1700s] — external
- Tubular or jacketed heat exchangers (still-worm condensers, brewery coolers) [1600s] — external/team-reachable
- Pressure gauges or U-tube manometers for empirical tuning [Hooke/Huygens, late 1600s] — external
- Crank-and-flywheel or beam drives capable of two synchronized cylinders [1700s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Boyle's Law: PV roughly constant at fixed T [1662] — external
- Empirical observation that compressed air heats up and that cooling between compressions reduces work [readily discoverable by experiment] — team-discoverable
- Qualitative caloric/heat-of-compression intuition [pre-1700 craft knowledge] — team-discoverable
- Concept of staging from distillation and multi-lift mine pumps [1600s] — external
Explanation: The compound air compressor is fundamentally an engineering rearrangement: take two force pumps of decreasing bore, pipe the discharge of the first through a water-cooled coil into the suction of the second, and add a receiver. Every component existed by the early 1700s in mining, brewing, and the nascent steam-engine trade, and Boyle's Law plus the obvious tactile fact that compressed air gets hot give the team all the conceptual scaffolding they need.
