The binding constraint is the bored cylinder with a gas-tight piston at modest pressures; before Newcomen-era cylinder boring matured around 1700–1715, leakage would defeat staging. Given that prerequisite, a motivated inventor plus a top artisan shop could plausibly build a working two-stage intercooled compressor by 1700–1730; a comfortably straightforward build awaits Smeaton/Wilkinson-grade boring and routine high-pressure receivers around 1760–1790, well before Mann's 1829 patent, driven more by industrial demand than missing technology.

possible_locations: England; France; Sweden; Belgium; Prussia/Germany; United States
geographic_note: The invention requires a mature iron-rolling industry capable of producing thin wrought-iron sheets at scale; Britain led decisively, but Sweden, France, and the US had competent rolling mills by the late 18th century. London's dockyards (where Palmer worked) provided the structural-engineering use case, but any major industrial port could host it.
full_text:
Invention: Corrugated galvanized iron (corrugated sheet iron)
Year actually invented: 1829
Earliest plausible: 1790–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1815–1828
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: London, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Sweden, Belgium, Prussia/Germany, United States
Geographic note: The invention requires a mature iron-rolling industry capable of producing thin wrought-iron sheets at scale; Britain led decisively, but Sweden, France, and the US had competent rolling mills by the late 18th century. London's dockyards (where Palmer worked) provided the structural-engineering use case, but any major industrial port could host it.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Rolling mills producing thin wrought-iron sheet [c. 1750s, Cort's grooved rolls 1783] — external
- Tinplate / sheet-iron industry (thin uniform sheets) [c. 1720s onward] — external
- Shaped/grooved rolls and pass-rolling for profiles [1783–1820] — external/team-reachable (one-layer precursor: fabricating matched corrugating rolls)
- Hand-powered rollers, presses, and drop-hammers for forming [18th c.] — external
- Rivets and structural fastening for sheet assemblies [ancient/refined 18th c.] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical fact that a corrugated/folded thin plate is far stiffer in bending than a flat one of equal mass [observable from paper, fabric pleating, classical fluted columns] — external/team-discoverable
- Qualitative beam/section theory [Parent 1713, Coulomb 1773, Euler] — external (helpful, not required)
- Observation that wrought iron sheet retains formed shape after cold or warm working [common forge knowledge] — external
Explanation: The binding constraint is the availability of reasonably uniform, thin wrought-iron sheet wide enough to corrugate; once Cort-style grooved/shaped rolling (1783) and a robust tinplate/sheet-iron trade exist, a motivated engineer with a skilled rolling-mill workshop can within five years cut matched corrugating rolls (a single precursor layer) and pass sheet through them, immediately observing the dramatic stiffness gain that pleated paper and fluted columns already demonstrate qualitatively. Earliest plausible is 1790–1810 because by then British and Swedish mills could supply the sheet and cut profiled rolls; the gap to 1829 is small and explained by the absence of a compelling structural-roofing market until early-19th-century dockyard, railway, and colonial-shed demand crystallized the use case — an economic/demand lag rather than a technical barrier.

possible_locations: Flag
geographic_note: Faraday's discovery occurred at the Royal Institution in London. Had it been team-reachable, candidate locations would mirror post-Oersted electromagnetic research hubs (London, Paris, Copenhagen, Munich), but the rubric forbids team derivation of this off-limits phenomenon.
full_text:
Invention: Electromagnetic induction
Year actually invented: 1831
Earliest plausible: Flag
Earliest straightforward: Flag
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: United Kingdom / London, United Kingdom
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Flag (off-limits phenomenon)
Geographic note: Faraday's discovery occurred at the Royal Institution in London, a leading center for electrical research. Had it been team-reachable, candidate locations would mirror post-Oersted electromagnetic research hubs (London, Paris, Copenhagen, Munich), but the rubric forbids team derivation.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / reliable current source [1800] — external (off-limits itself; assumed extant as published knowledge after 1800)
- Insulated copper wire and helical coil winding [c. 1820] — team-reachable
- Soft iron cores and permanent magnets [ancient / refined c. 1820s] — team-reachable
- Sensitive galvanometer (Schweigger multiplier) [1820] — team-reachable
- Mechanical rotation/commutator hardware [1820s] — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Oersted's discovery: electric current deflects a magnetic needle [1820] — external (off-limits)
- Ampere's law of forces between currents [1820–1826] — external
- Arago's rotating-disk effect (induced eddy currents in copper) [1824] — external (a serendipitous adjacent observation)
- Concept that a CHANGING magnetic flux induces an EMF in a nearby conductor [1831] — off-limits (the discovery itself)
- Field-line / "lines of force" conceptual framework [Faraday, 1830s] — new scientific framework, off-limits
Explanation: This entry is FLAGGED because electromagnetic induction is explicitly on the off-limits list — it is a foundational electromagnetic phenomenon, not an engineering artifact. By 1830 the team would have had voltaic piles, galvanometers, electromagnets, insulated coils, and Oersted's and Ampere's results in hand, plus Arago's puzzling rotating-disk effect. The binding item, however, is not hardware but the scientific insight that only a CHANGING magnetic flux (not a static field) induces an EMF in a circuit. Discovering this required Faraday's deliberate, theory-guided experiments with transient effects and the new conceptual framework of lines of force — exactly the kind of controlled scientific investigation and framework creation that engineers iterating on devices cannot be expected to produce. Static configurations of magnets and coils give null results, so empirical workshop tinkering aimed at building useful devices would not surface the effect; it had to be hunted for by someone already hypothesizing it. Hence no earliest-plausible engineering date can be assigned.

possible_locations: England; Scotland; France; Belgium; Prussia; United States
geographic_note: The decisive precursor was the textile nap-shearing machine developed in English wool-finishing centers (Gloucestershire, Yorkshire), so southwest England is by far the most likely birthplace; other locations require an independent textile-engineering culture with similar rotary-shear practice.
full_text:
Invention: Lawn mower
Year actually invented: 1830
Earliest plausible: 1815–1830
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1830
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: England / Stroud, Gloucestershire, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, Scotland, France, Belgium, Prussia, United States
Geographic note: The decisive precursor was the textile nap-shearing machine developed in English wool-finishing centers (Gloucestershire, Yorkshire), so southwest England is by far the most likely birthplace; other locations require an independent textile-engineering culture with similar rotary-shear practice.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Precision wrought-iron gear trains and cast-iron framing for small machines [c. 1790–1810] — external (mature in Britain via millwrighting and early machine-tool work)
- Helical/spiral cutter reel geometry running against a fixed bed knife [c. 1815, Lewis’s cloth-shearing machine, patented 1815] — external (this is the single load-bearing precursor; pre-1700 hand shears and scythes do not embody a reel-against-ledger geometry, and no documented rotary helical cutting mechanism predates the napping/shearing machines of the 1790s–1815, so the team cannot reach it without that one-layer precursor)
- Roller-and-axle drive with bevel/spur gearing to step up cutter speed [c. 1800] — team-reachable (standard millwright practice, one-layer build)
- Cast-iron rollers and light wheeled chassis [c. 1800] — external (foundry practice well established)
- Hardened steel cutting edges with grindable bed knife [pre-1800] — external (edge-tool trades)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical observation that a helical blade sweeping past a fixed straight edge produces a continuous scissoring cut along the blade’s length [c. 1815] — team-discoverable, but only via the textile-shearing precedent
- Observation that grass, like wool nap, can be cut cleanly by a scissoring action rather than impact (scythe) or tearing [team-discoverable] — empirical, achievable in a five-year iteration once the reel exists
- No new scientific framework required; purely mechanical
Explanation: The binding item is the helical cutter-reel-against-ledger-blade geometry, which is not a generic “scissor” idea but a specific kinematic configuration first realized in cloth-shearing machines (notably John Lewis’s 1815 patent and its immediate predecessors in the 1790s nap-shearing trade); pre-1700 cutting tools (scythes, hand shears, sickles, even pole-lathe cutters) do not exhibit a rotating helical edge sweeping a stationary straight edge, so the team cannot reach the reel concept without that one allowed precursor layer. Once the textile shear existed, Budding’s leap was essentially analogical — apply the cloth-finisher’s reel to turf — and the supporting technologies (cast iron, gearing, rollers) were all in hand by 1815, giving a plausible window of 1815–1830 and a straightforward window of 1820–1830 once the textile machine was in widespread workshop use. The 15-year gap from 1815 to 1830 (rather than a 130-year gap) is short and historically reasonable: it took roughly that long for the shearing-frame trade to mature, for an inventor embedded in a Gloucestershire cloth mill (Budding himself) to see both machines daily, and for ornamental lawns — driven by Repton/Loudon-era landscape fashion and growing middle-class villa gardens — to become a market worth serving rather than a job for scythemen and grazing sheep. Before 1815 the reel geometry simply did not exist in any documented machine, so the earlier 1700–1815 interval fails the one-layer rule.

actual_location: Königsberg, Prussia (later demonstrated in St. Petersburg, Russia)
possible_locations: Prussia (any city, including Königsberg or Berlin); United Kingdom (London); France (Paris); Russia (St. Petersburg); Denmark (Copenhagen)
geographic_note: ALIGNED. Königsberg was a Prussian university city with access to electromagnetic research circulating across German states, Britain, France, and Russia in the 1820s–30s; the prerequisite stack (batteries, wire, machined iron cores, commutators) was reproducible in any of these centers.
full_text:
Invention: First practical rotating electric motor
Year actually invented: 1834
Earliest plausible: 1822–1828
Earliest straightforward: 1830–1834
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Königsberg, Prussia (later demonstrated in St. Petersburg, Russia)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Prussia (any city, including Königsberg or Berlin), United Kingdom (London), France (Paris), Russia (St. Petersburg), Denmark (Copenhagen)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Königsberg was a Prussian university city with access to electromagnetic research circulating across German states, Britain, France, and Russia in the 1820s–30s; the prerequisite stack (batteries, wire, machined iron cores, commutators) was reproducible in any of these centers. Jacobi himself moved between Prussia and Russia, illustrating the cross-border accessibility of the relevant know-how.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / scaled battery banks [1800, scaled by ~1820s] — external (Volta’s pile public 1800; by the 1820s Daniell-style and large multi-cell arrays were within artisan reach, and the team can build ONE LAYER of larger battery bank to deliver the sustained current needed for boat propulsion vs. a tabletop demo)
- Electromagnet with iron core (Sturgeon 1825; Henry’s stronger insulated-wire versions 1827–1831) [1825–1831] — external (the leap from Jedlik’s 1828 demo to a “practical” motor depends on Henry-style multilayer insulated windings yielding far greater lifting force; team-reachable as one layer of refinement in winding technique)
- Insulated copper wire / silk-wrapping [c. 1820s] — external (artisan workshop capability)
- Mechanical commutator / brush contacts [c. 1832, Pixii; conceptually present in Jedlik 1828] — team-reachable (a motivated workshop can iterate brush/segment geometry empirically; refinement for higher current and continuous load is the key delta from a demo motor)
- Precision machining of shafts, bearings, iron pole pieces [pre-1820, mature] — external
- Galvanic chemistry for sustained current (zinc-copper, later Daniell 1836) [1800–1836] — external; pre-Daniell large Wollaston-type batteries suffice as one layer
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Oersted’s electromagnetism (1820) — external (OFF-LIMITS as team discovery; must be public, so earliest plausible cannot precede 1820)
- Ampère’s force law / solenoid behavior (1820–1826) — external
- Faraday’s rotation experiment (1821) — external (demonstrated continuous electromagnetic rotation, the conceptual seed)
- Empirical knowledge that current scales magnetic force [pre-1825] — team-discoverable through iteration
Explanation: The binding constraint is Oersted’s 1820 discovery (off-limits as team discovery), which sets the floor; Faraday’s 1821 rotation and Sturgeon’s 1825 electromagnet then make a rotating motor conceivable, and Jedlik’s 1828 device proves a commutated EM motor was achievable with that stack. What separates Jacobi’s 1834 motor as “first practical” is not new physics but engineering scale: substantially larger battery banks to sustain useful current, Henry-style multilayer insulated windings producing far stronger electromagnets, and a more robust commutator able to handle higher currents under continuous mechanical load — enough to propel a boat rather than spin a laboratory armature. All three are empirical refinements (one layer of precursor tech) reachable by a skilled artisan workshop given five years; therefore the earliest plausible window is 1822–1828, with the straightforward window 1830–1834 once Henry-class electromagnets and Pixii-style commutators were in public circulation.

possible_locations: England; France; Italy; Netherlands; German states; Spain; Ottoman Empire; China
geographic_note: Any literate society with a standardized alphabet, printing/tabulation traditions, and an interest in long-distance signaling (military, naval, or cryptographic) could have produced such a code. European powers in the early modern period had the strongest combined incentives (naval signaling, ciphers, postal networks).
full_text:
Invention: Morse Code
Year actually invented: 1837
Earliest plausible: 1600–1650
Earliest straightforward: 1790–1810
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: United States / New York, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Italy, Netherlands, German states, Spain, Ottoman Empire, China
Geographic note: Any literate society with a standardized alphabet, printing/tabulation traditions, and an interest in long-distance signaling (military, naval, or cryptographic) could have produced such a code. European powers in the early modern period had the strongest combined incentives (naval signaling, ciphers, postal networks).
Prerequisite technologies:
- Standardized written alphabet [ancient] — external
- Pen, paper, and printing for codebook dissemination [c. 1450] — external
- Some signaling medium with two distinguishable durations or states [ancient] — external/team-reachable
- Mechanical timekeeping sufficient to distinguish “short” vs “long” intervals [ancient–1656] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Letter-frequency analysis (shorter codes for commoner letters) — known to Arab cryptographers [c. 9th c., al-Kindi]; well established in European cryptography [c. 1466, Alberti] — external
- Polybius square / biliteral encoding (Bacon’s cipher, 1605) demonstrating any letter can be encoded as a sequence from a small symbol set [c. 150 BCE; 1605] — external
- Empirical observation that operators can reliably distinguish two signal durations — team-discoverable in days
Explanation: Morse Code is a notational invention requiring no new physics, no precision manufacturing, and no scientific framework beyond ideas already mature by the early 17th century. Francis Bacon’s 1605 biliteral cipher explicitly encoded the alphabet as 5-symbol sequences drawn from a 2-element set, which is conceptually identical to Morse; al-Kindi’s 9th-century frequency analysis supplied the optimization principle that distinguishes Morse from a fixed-length code. The binding constraint is therefore the conceptual leap of pairing a variable-length binary code with a real-time signaling medium (lantern, drum, bell, or shutter semaphore), all of which existed in antiquity. A motivated team in c. 1610 — backed by a naval power or a cryptographer-prince — could design and field-test such a code within five years. The 1790–1810 “straightforward” window reflects the era of optical semaphore networks (Chappe, 1792), when the practical demand for fast alphabetic signaling made a Morse-like code an obvious next step.

full_text:
Invention: Electrotyping
Year actually invented: 1838
Earliest plausible: 1800–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1810–1820
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Russia / St. Petersburg, Russia
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (London), France (Paris), Italy (Pavia/Como), German states, Russia (St. Petersburg)
Geographic note: Requires access to a working voltaic pile and a skilled metalworking/printing workshop, concentrated in early-19th-century European scientific capitals where Volta’s pile was being replicated within months of its 1800 announcement.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile [1800] — external (off-limits as team discovery, usable as external prerequisite post-1800)
- Copper sulfate solution (blue vitriol, known since antiquity) [pre-1700] — external
- Graphite (plumbago) for conductive coating [pre-1700] — external
- Wax/plaster molding for typefounding and medal casting [pre-1700] — external
- Copper anode/sheet metallurgy [pre-1700] — team-reachable
- Glass/ceramic vessels and simple wiring [pre-1800] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Electrolytic decomposition of metallic salts (Nicholson & Carlisle 1800; Cruickshank’s deposition of copper and silver from solution 1800) [1800] — external
- Empirical observation that deposited metal conforms to cathode surface [1800–1805] — team-discoverable
- Faraday’s laws of electrolysis [1833] — not strictly required; empirical control of current and bath concentration suffices
Explanation: The binding constraint is the voltaic pile (1800); every other prerequisite predates 1800 by centuries. Within months of Volta’s announcement, Cruickshank had already deposited copper onto cathodes from sulfate solutions, meaning the core empirical phenomenon was in hand by late 1800. A motivated inventor with a skilled workshop, given a pile and Cruickshank’s observation, would need only to coat a non-conductive mold with graphite and iterate bath chemistry and current duration to grow a coherent shell — both well within empirical reach without Faraday’s quantitative laws. The 38-year historical gap reflects not technical difficulty but the slow diffusion of electrochemistry as a curiosity plus the weakness and inconvenience of pile-based current before Daniell’s constant cell (1836).

full_text:
Invention: Steam Shovel
Year actually invented: 1839
Earliest plausible: 1800–1815
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1835
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Canton, Massachusetts, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Britain (Manchester, Birmingham, Newcastle, London), USA (Philadelphia, Boston), France (Paris)
Geographic note: Requires a heavy-engineering culture with stationary steam engines, ironworks, and large civil-engineering projects (canals, mines, railways). Britain is the most likely birthplace given Boulton & Watt and the Cornish/Newcastle engine-building traditions; the eastern USA is a close second by the 1820s.
Prerequisite technologies:
- High-pressure portable/semi-portable steam engine [c. 1802, Trevithick] — external
- Cast and wrought iron structural members; iron chain and gearing [18th c.] — external
- Crank, gear train, clutch, and friction-brake hoisting mechanisms (mine winding gear) [18th c.] — external
- Boom/jib cranes with slewing platforms (dockyard and shipyard cranes) [18th c.] — external
- Railway truck/flanged-wheel chassis on rails [c. 1800–1820, colliery tramways] — external
- Boilers capable of mobile/field service [c. 1800–1810] — external
- Bucket/dipper digging implements (dredgers, bucket-chain excavators) [17th–18th c.] — external
- Wire/iron hoisting rope and pulleys [18th c.] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical thermodynamics of steam [pre-1800] — external
- Practical statics of beams and booms under load [18th c.] — external
- Soil resistance/digging-force empirics from canal and mining work [18th c.] — team-discoverable
Explanation: Every functional element — a steam engine, a slewing boom, a dipper arm with crowd motion, hoisting gear, and a rail chassis — existed independently by roughly 1810. The binding constraint is a sufficiently compact, robust, mobile steam engine that can drive intermittent shock loads; Trevithick’s high-pressure engines (c. 1802) are the one-layer precursor. A motivated inventor with a skilled workshop, drawing on existing dockyard slewing cranes and colliery winding engines mounted on tramway trucks, could plausibly have assembled a working steam shovel by 1810–1815, though the absence of strong economic pull (large-scale railway earthworks only arrived in the late 1820s) explains why the straightforward date slips to 1820–1835.

possible_locations: Britain (Manchester/Birmingham/Sheffield/Soho); possibly France (Paris/Le Creusot)
geographic_note: Only Britain in the 1790s combined high-pressure-capable steam engine workshops (Boulton & Watt, Wilkinson) with large-scale iron forging practice; Le Creusot in France is a plausible secondary site.
full_text:
Invention: Steam Hammer
Year actually invented: 1839
Earliest plausible: 1790–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1800–1815
Confidence: High
Actual location: Patricroft, England, UK
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Britain (Manchester, Birmingham, Sheffield, Soho/Birmingham), possibly France (Paris/Le Creusot)
Geographic note: Only Britain in the 1790s combined high-pressure-capable steam engine workshops (Boulton & Watt, Wilkinson) with large-scale iron forging practice; Le Creusot in France is a plausible secondary site.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Watt double-acting steam engine with separate condenser [1769–1782] — external
- Slide valves / D-valve for steam admission and exhaust [1799, Murray; team-reachable earlier] — team-reachable
- Wilkinson’s precision boring mill for cylinders [1774] — external
- Cast-iron framing and large anvil block casting [1770s–1780s] — external
- Trip-hammer / helve-hammer forging practice and guide-frame construction [pre-1700] — external
- Piston rod, crosshead, and guide-pillar linear motion hardware [1780s] — external
- Stuffing boxes and packing capable of moderate steam pressure [1780s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical knowledge that steam pressure scales force linearly with piston area [pre-1770] — external
- Empirical impact-energy intuition (mass times drop height) from trip-hammer practice [pre-1700] — external
- No new scientific framework required
Explanation: The binding item is the Watt double-acting steam engine with reliable cylinder boring and slide-valve steam distribution, firmly in place by the late 1780s. Once a workshop can build a vertical double-acting cylinder with a piston rod guided in line, inverting it over an anvil and letting the rod carry a hammer head is a straightforward mechanical rearrangement — the team needs only iterate on valve timing for controllable single blows, guide-pillar alignment, and a sufficiently massive anvil block. A motivated Boulton & Watt-class workshop in 1790–1800 could plausibly have built one to solve the real problem of forging large paddle shafts and crank axles; the historical 40-year gap reflects demand pull (large marine and locomotive forgings only became urgent in the 1830s with Brunel’s Great Britain shaft) rather than any missing technical capability.

full_text:
Invention: Photovoltaic effect
Year actually invented: 1839
Earliest plausible: Flag
Earliest straightforward: Flag
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: France / Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Paris (France), London (UK), Berlin (Prussia)
Geographic note: Becquerel’s discovery occurred in his father’s electrochemical laboratory in Paris. Had it been team-reachable, candidate locations would mirror post-Oersted electromagnetic research hubs, but the rubric forbids team derivation.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / electrochemical cell [1800] — external
- Sensitive galvanometer [1820s] — external
- Platinum/silver electrodes and dilute acid electrolytes [1800s] — external
- Glass apparatus and silver halide coatings [1830s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Photovoltaic effect itself (light-induced voltage at electrolyte/electrode junction) [1839] — NOT team-discoverable (off-limits)
- Quantum/photon theory of light absorption [1905] — not available
- Semiconductor band theory [1930s] — not available
Explanation: The photovoltaic effect is explicitly on the off-limits list. Although every physical prerequisite (voltaic cells, galvanometers, platinum electrodes, acidic electrolytes, silver halide chemistry) was in hand by the 1820s, recognizing that illumination of one electrode produces a measurable current is a serendipitous observation that also requires a new conceptual framework (light interacting with matter to liberate charge carriers). Without the off-limits scientific discovery, the team has no reason to illuminate an electrode and attribute observed current to light rather than temperature, contamination, or instrument noise. FLAG.

possible_locations: United Kingdom (London/Manchester); France (Paris); Germany (Berlin); United States (New York/Philadelphia/Boston)
geographic_note: ALIGNED. The USA was a plausible location by the late 1820s given active scientific correspondence networks and Henry's own electromagnetic work, though the strongest concentration of skilled instrument-makers and electromagnet experimenters in the 1827–1832 window was in Britain and continental Europe.
full_text:
Invention: Electromechanical relay
Year actually invented: 1835
Earliest plausible: 1827–1832
Earliest straightforward: 1830–1835
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Albany, New York, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (London/Manchester), France (Paris), Germany (Berlin), United States (New York/Philadelphia/Boston)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The USA was a plausible location by the late 1820s given active scientific correspondence networks and Henry's own electromagnetic work, though the strongest concentration of skilled instrument-makers and electromagnet experimenters in the 1827–1832 window was in Britain and continental Europe.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Insulated wire (varnished/silk-wound) [pre-1820] — external/team-reachable (silk-wound wire was a known craft technique; the team can adapt it for electrical use by empirical iteration)
- Volta's wet-cell battery [1800] — external/team-reachable (explicitly listed as usable external knowledge; provides the controllable DC current source)
- Sturgeon's electromagnet (soft-iron core, bare copper coil) [1825] — external/team-reachable (published in Transactions of the Society of Arts 1825; disseminated widely by 1827; the team can reproduce and improve it in one empirical layer)
- Mechanical armature / pivoted lever (clock/balance-wheel craft) [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable (standard clockmaker and instrument-maker skill)
- Spring-loaded or gravity-return mechanism [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable (routine in lock and clockwork craft)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Oersted's discovery: current-carrying wire deflects compass needle [1820] — external (off-limits as team discovery; disseminated by late 1820, widely reprinted by 1821, giving several years of lead time before the 1827 floor)
- Ampère's mathematical and experimental work on solenoids and mutual attraction of current loops [1820–1822] — external/team-reachable (published findings; team can use empirical observations without the mathematics)
- Arago and Davy: iron becomes temporarily magnetic inside a current-carrying coil [1820–1821] — external/team-reachable (demonstrated publicly and published; establishes the soft-iron temporary-magnet principle the relay depends on)
- Sturgeon's demonstration that a varnished-wire multi-turn coil dramatically increases lifting force [1825] — external/team-reachable (the key empirical step transforming Arago's curiosity into a practical actuator; dissemination gives a realistic knowledge floor of ~1826–1827)
Explanation: The binding constraint is the practical electromagnet capable of reliably actuating a mechanical armature at a distance. Oersted's 1820 result is off-limits as a team discovery and must fully pre-date the plausible window; with dissemination taking roughly a year, Arago's and Davy's temporary-magnetism demonstrations reach the community by 1821, but Sturgeon's critical improvement — the multi-turn insulated-wire coil producing a strongly attracting soft-iron core — was not published until 1825 and not widely replicated until roughly 1826–1827. A motivated team starting in 1827 could reproduce Sturgeon's electromagnet in one empirical layer, then straightforwardly mount a pivoted soft-iron armature on a return spring and connect it to a local circuit, yielding the relay function. The floor of 1827 therefore reflects the minimum realistic dissemination time for Sturgeon's result; a team working at the cutting edge in London or Paris could plausibly close the remaining design steps within one to five years, giving 1827–1832 as the earliest plausible range. The straightforward window of 1830–1835 reflects the additional time needed for the concept of remote circuit switching (rather than mere attraction demonstration) to be recognized as a useful goal, which historically required the telegraph development context.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; United States
geographic_note: Goodyear's discovery occurred in the USA, which is among the plausible locations at the earliest plausible date. ALIGNED.
full_text:
Invention: Vulcanized Rubber
Year actually invented: 1839
Earliest plausible: 1800–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1835
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: Woburn, Massachusetts, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, United States
Geographic note: Goodyear's discovery occurred in the USA, which is among the plausible locations at the earliest plausible date. ALIGNED.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Natural rubber (latex) harvesting and importation [pre-1750] — external (Amazonian rubber known to Europeans by mid-18th century; commercially imported by 1750s)
- Sulfur as a processed material (mined and refined elemental sulfur) [pre-1700] — external (sulfur long used in gunpowder, medicine, and vulcanization precursor experiments)
- Mechanical rubber masticator / mixing mill [pre-1795] — external (earlier textile and metalworking roll mills of the late 18th century — e.g., Arkwright-era roller presses, 1780s — provide sufficient mechanical precedent; the team could adapt these before 1800, placing this clearly before the plausible window floor)
- Controlled-temperature heating apparatus (iron stove, oven with thermometric monitoring) [pre-1790] — external (cast-iron stoves and Wedgwood pyrometers available by 1780s)
- Rubber-solvent processing (turpentine or naphtha dissolution for compound mixing) [pre-1790] — external (solvent chemistry and distillation well established)
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical knowledge that raw rubber becomes tacky in heat and brittle in cold [pre-1800] — external (widely known commercial complaint by 1790s; motivates search for stabilization)
- Observation that sulfur alters the properties of organic materials under heat [pre-1800] — external/team-discoverable (sulfur's reactivity with organics known from alchemical and early chemical tradition; a directed team would include sulfur in an initial parameter sweep)
- No requirement for polymer chemistry theory — crosslinking mechanism is entirely post-hoc; empirical outcome is self-evident to any artisan evaluating samples
Explanation: The serendipity flag is warranted and must be addressed directly. Goodyear's critical step — dropping a rubber-sulfur compound onto a hot stove and observing charring with a non-tacky elastic border — was an accident, not a planned experiment. To arrive at the same result without the accident, a directed 5-year team would need to run a systematic heat-plus-sulfur parameter sweep across a meaningful temperature range. The binding constraint is that the effective vulcanization window is approximately 130–180°C: too cool and crosslinking is incomplete; too hot and the rubber degrades. With only empirical thermometry available (no thermocouple, relying on Wedgwood pyrometers or boiling-point references), reliably hitting and recognizing this window is non-trivial but not implausible — a team systematically testing sulfur-loaded rubber samples against graded heat sources (open flame, steam, boiling oil at ~200°C, sand baths) over five years would likely traverse this range. The earliest plausible date is set at 1800–1810 rather than 1780 to ensure the rubber masticator/mixing capability clearly pre-dates the window floor: by 1795–1800, adapted textile roll mills provide sufficient precursor capability with time to spare. The earliest straightforward date of 1820–1835 reflects the period when Hancock's dedicated masticator, improved rubber imports, and broader artisan awareness of rubber's temperature-sensitivity problems all converge to make a directed effort highly likely to succeed within five years without requiring any accident.

possible_locations: France; United Kingdom; Germany (German states); Switzerland; United States
geographic_note: ALIGNED. Paris and London were the principal centers for camera obscura optics, silver chemistry, and the experimental milieu (Niépce, Wedgwood, Davy) that produced photographic experimentation in the early 19th century.
full_text:
Invention: Daguerreotype
Year actually invented: 1839
Earliest plausible: 1815–1825
Earliest straightforward: 1830–1840
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: France / Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, United Kingdom, Germany (German states), Switzerland, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Paris and London were the principal centers for camera obscura optics, silver chemistry, and the experimental milieu (Niépce, Wedgwood, Davy) that produced photographic experimentation in the early 19th century.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Camera obscura with quality lens [c. 1600s; achromatic doublets 1758] — external/team-reachable
- Silvered/Sheffield-plate copper plates [1740s] — external/team-reachable
- Elemental iodine (and iodine vapor handling) [1811, Courtois] — external/team-reachable
- Elemental mercury and controlled vapor heating [ancient; lab use established] — external/team-reachable
- Sodium thiosulfate ("hyposulfite") as silver halide solvent [Chaussier 1799; Herschel identified fixing role 1819] — external/team-reachable
- Common salt (NaCl) brine fixing alternative [ancient] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Silver salts darken on light exposure [Schulze 1727] — external/team-discoverable
- Silver halides (AgCl, AgI) are strongly photosensitive [late 18th c.; Wedgwood/Davy 1802] — external/team-discoverable
- Hyposulfite dissolves silver halides, enabling permanent fixing [Herschel 1819; empirically discoverable earlier] — external/team-discoverable
- Mercury amalgamates preferentially with light-exposed silver iodide grains (latent-image development) — empirically discoverable but non-obvious — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint is iodine, isolated only in 1811; before that there is no route to silver iodide, the specific halide whose sensitivity and mercury-developable latent image make the daguerreotype practical. Once iodine is available, a motivated inventor with a skilled workshop has every other ingredient in hand: polished silvered copper (Sheffield plate), a good camera obscura with an achromatic lens, mercury, and either salt brine or hyposulfite (known since 1799, recognized as a silver-halide solvent by 1819) for fixing. The genuinely non-obvious step is discovering that mercury vapor develops a latent image on lightly exposed silver iodide — this is empirical but reachable within a five-year iterative program comparing halides (Cl, Br, I), exposure regimes, and post-treatments, exactly the search Niépce and Daguerre actually performed. Hence an earliest plausible window of roughly 1815–1825 (immediately after iodine becomes available and a team could begin systematic halide trials), with a straightforward window of 1830–1840 matching the historical date.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Germany; Sweden; Netherlands
geographic_note: ALIGNED. By the 1820s–1830s, iron-cyanide chemistry was being actively developed in Britain, France, and the German states; any well-equipped chemical workshop in these regions could pursue the process.
full_text:
Invention: Blueprint (cyanotype) process
Year actually invented: 1840
Earliest plausible: 1825–1835
Earliest straightforward: 1835–1845
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: United Kingdom / Slough, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Germany, Sweden, Netherlands
Geographic note: ALIGNED. By the 1820s–1830s, iron-cyanide chemistry was being actively developed in Britain, France, and the German states; any well-equipped chemical workshop in these regions could pursue the process.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Prussian blue pigment [1704] — external/team-reachable
- Iron(III) ammonium citrate (ferric salts of organic acids) [early 1800s] — external/team-reachable
- Potassium ferricyanide (Gmelin's red prussiate) [1822] — external/team-reachable
- Sized/coatable paper for chemical impregnation [18th c.] — external/team-reachable
- Glass-plate contact printing frames (from heliography practice) [c.1820s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Photosensitivity of silver salts (general awareness that some salts darken under light, motivating systematic testing of other metallic salts) [Schulze 1727; Scheele 1777] — external/team-discoverable
- Photoreduction of iron(III) to iron(II) by UV/sunlight in presence of organic ligands (citrate, oxalate) [empirically discoverable] — team-discoverable
- Insolubility of ferric ferrocyanide (Prussian blue) and reaction of Fe(II) with ferricyanide to form it [known iron-cyanide chemistry, 1820s] — external/team-reachable
Explanation: The binding constraint is the availability of potassium ferricyanide (Gmelin, 1822); before that, the specific Fe(II)+ferricyanide → Prussian blue reaction underlying cyanotype cannot be exploited. Once ferricyanide exists and ferric ammonium citrate is accessible, the photosensitivity of iron-organic salts is straightforwardly discoverable by an empirical workshop: a motivated chemist coating papers with various metal salts and exposing them in sunlight would readily notice ferric citrate solutions bleaching/reducing under sun. Combining the two solutions and washing then becomes an iterative engineering problem rather than a scientific leap. Earliest plausible therefore tracks shortly after 1822; earliest straightforward sits closer to the actual 1840 date because the motivating context (photography) only crystallized after Daguerre/Talbot in 1839.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Italy; German states; Denmark; Netherlands
geographic_note: ALIGNED. Britain had the precision clockmaking, electrochemistry expertise, and instrument-making workshops needed; France and the German states were equally plausible given Volta's pile and active galvanic experimentation across Europe.
full_text:
Invention: Printing telegraph
Year actually invented: 1841
Earliest plausible: 1810–1815
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1825
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: United Kingdom / Edinburgh, Scotland (later London)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Italy, German states, Denmark, Netherlands
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Britain had the precision clockmaking, electrochemistry expertise, and instrument-making workshops needed; France and the German states were equally plausible given Volta's pile and active galvanic experimentation across Europe.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / galvanic battery [1800] — external
- Insulated copper wire and basic circuit hardware [c. 1800] — external
- Precision pendulum/spring clocks (Harrison, Mudge, Arnold) [c. 1760–1790] — external
- Make-break key / mechanical contact switches [pre-1800, trivial] — team-reachable
- Chemically-impregnated paper (potassium ferrocyanide and iron salts; Prussian blue chemistry known since 1706; iodide/starch since 1814) [pre-1815] — external
- Paper tape transport mechanism (clockwork-driven) [c. 1800] — team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Electrolysis / electrochemical decomposition by current (Nicholson & Carlisle) [1800] — external
- Observation that current through ferrocyanide/iodide-impregnated paper produces a visible colored mark — team-discoverable empirically (one-layer precursor, not requiring Oersted)
- Pendulum synchronization principles (Huygens) [1657] — external
Explanation: The binding item is the voltaic pile (1800), which is off-limits as a team discovery but freely usable as external knowledge once published. Critically, this invention does NOT depend on Oersted (1820) because chemical/electrolytic marking uses the direct chemical action of current, not magnetic deflection. A motivated inventor with a skilled clockmaking and chemistry workshop in roughly 1810–1815 could empirically iterate on chemically-impregnated papers, find a salt that marks visibly under a weak current, couple a simple make-break key to a long wire, and synchronize transmitter and receiver via matched pendulum clocks. The straightforward window slips to 1820–1825 once Ampère/Oersted-era electrical instrumentation and Ronalds-style telegraph experience make the integration routine.

A motivated team in 1810–1815 could fabricate platinum-black or platinized foil empirically and build a needle-deflection detector as a one-layer precursor, making 1810–1820 plausible; a more comfortable, well-instrumented version places the straightforward window at 1825–1835, still well before Grove's 1842 demonstration.

full_text:
Invention: Modern high-strength Portland cement
Year actually invented: 1845
Earliest plausible: 1790–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1840
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Kent, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Germany, Belgium
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in Kent, England, which had the industrial kiln infrastructure, active lime/cement trade, and empirical engineering culture most likely to produce this innovation at any plausible early date. Continental Europe (France, Germany) had comparable refractory kiln technology and building trades but slightly less concentrated commercial cement experimentation.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Reverberatory and bottle kilns capable of sustained ~1450°C — external [~1780s, English and continental iron/ceramics industries]
- Systematic lime burning and slaking practice — external [ancient, well-documented by 1700s]
- Parker's "Roman cement" / natural hydraulic cement (calcined argillaceous limestone) — external [1796, England]
- Refractory brick lining for high-temperature kilns — external [late 18th century, English pottery/iron industries]
- Proportioned limestone + clay blending as feedstock — team-reachable [derivable empirically from natural cement observations by ~1800]
- Grinding and screening of clinker — external [millstone technology, extant]

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Observation that overburned lime clinker (previously discarded as waste) produces harder, denser set — team-discoverable [empirically accessible once high-temperature kilns exist, ~1790s onward]
- Hydraulicity linked to clay/silica content in limestone — external [Smeaton's empirical observations, ~1756; Vicat's systematic work, 1818]
- Calcium silicate phase formation as mechanism of strength — NOT required (team operates empirically; mechanism was not understood until late 19th century)
- Relationship between calcination temperature and product strength — team-discoverable [straightforward empirical iteration with thermometric instruments, ~1800s]

Explanation: The binding constraint is sustained kiln temperature of approximately 1450°C combined with the empirical insight that previously discarded vitrified clinker is the high-value product rather than a defect. High-temperature reverberatory kilns capable of approaching this range existed in the English ceramics and iron industries by the 1780s–1790s, and Parker's 1796 natural hydraulic cement had already demonstrated that argillaceous limestones produce hydraulic binders. A motivated team in possession of these kilns, aware of Parker's cement and Smeaton's hydraulicity observations, could empirically iterate toward higher burn temperatures and test the resulting clinker for set strength—exactly the path Johnson followed, but without needing Aspdin's 1824 intermediate step as a prerequisite. The earliest plausible window (1790–1810) requires only extant kiln technology and the empirical observation about clinker strength, both reachable without new science. The earliest straightforward window (1820–1840) reflects the additional value of Vicat's 1818 systematic cement chemistry and the broader commercial context that made the problem economically salient. No serendipitous accident is required; the innovation is a deliberate empirical push to higher temperatures, fully within engineering reach once high-temperature industrial kilns are available.

full_text:
Invention: Tunnel-boring machine
Year actually invented: 1846

Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Belgium / France (Mont Cenis / Fréjus tunnel project)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Belgium, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in the France/Belgium sphere, which had both the industrial base and the pressing civil-engineering demand (Alpine rail tunneling) to motivate the work. The UK was equally or better positioned industrially and had the most active tunneling culture of the era.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Rotary drilling / boring (well-boring, mine drilling) [pre-1700] — external/team-reachable
- Watt/Boulton condensing steam engine (compact, high-pressure variants) [1769–1800] — external/team-reachable
- High-pressure steam engine (Trevithick) [1800–1804] — external/team-reachable
- Brunel's tunneling shield (segmented face support, sequential advance) [1818] — external/team-reachable; binding lower bound for combined concept
- Wrought-iron and cast-iron structural fabrication at scale [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Mechanical power transmission (gears, crankshafts, shafting) [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Compressed-air prime movers and piping [~1800–1820] — external/team-reachable
- Conveyor / spoil-removal mechanisms (chain, screw, bucket) [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Rock mechanics (empirical: hardness, fracture under point load) [pre-1800] — team-discoverable through iterative trial
- Steam thermodynamics (Watt, Carnot) [1769–1824] — external; full theory not required, empirical steam practice sufficient
- Empirical observation that rotary cutting outperforms percussive drilling in certain lithologies [team-discoverable within 5-year program]

Explanation: The binding lower bound is Brunel's tunneling shield of 1818, because Maus's machine integrates the shield's face-support and sequential-advance concept with a mechanized rotary cutter — treating the shield as the one permitted layer of precursor technology. Without it, a rotary cutter advancing unsupported through a soft or fractured Alpine face is not viable, and inventing the shield concept simultaneously would constitute chaining. By 1818 every other prerequisite — high-pressure steam engines, robust iron fabrication, rotary drilling practice, and spoil-removal mechanisms — was already available and team-reachable. A motivated inventor with a skilled engineering workshop, given the shield as prior art, could plausibly have designed and tested a powered rotary TBM in the range 1818–1825; the straightforward window of 1830–1840 reflects the additional time needed for compact, reliable underground steam or compressed-air drive trains to mature to a point where iterative field testing was practical without unacceptable hazard. The gap between earliest plausible (~1818) and actual (1846) is roughly 25–28 years, well under the 100-year threshold, and is explained by the absence of a compelling immediate application: no railway tunnel of sufficient length and hardness existed as a forcing function until the Mont Cenis project created the demand that motivated Maus's design.

full_text:
Invention: Nitroglycerin
Year actually invented: 1847
Earliest plausible: 1838–1845
Earliest straightforward: 1843–1847
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: Turin, Italy
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, Germany, United Kingdom, Italy, Sweden
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Turin was a credible center of chemical research in the 1840s, and Sobrero worked under Pelouze in Paris before returning — the invention sits squarely in the French-Italian-German chemical research corridor that dominated organic chemistry at the time.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Concentrated sulfuric acid (oil of vitriol, industrial production) [ancient / well-established by 1800] — external/team-reachable
- Concentrated nitric acid (distillation from saltpeter + sulfuric acid) [well-established by 1700s] — external/team-reachable
- Glycerol (isolated by Scheele via saponification of fats) [1779] — external/team-reachable
- Laboratory glassware for controlled dropwise addition and cold-bath temperature management [well-established by 1800] — external/team-reachable
- Mixed acid nitration technique (nitric + sulfuric acid as nitrating agent) [early 1800s, formalized by Pelouze and contemporaries by ~1838] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Organic chemistry as a systematic discipline; concept of substitution/addition reactions on organic substrates [1830s, Dumas, Liebig, Pelouze] — external/team-discoverable
- Guncotton / nitrocellulose synthesis demonstrating nitration of polyfunctional organic molecules produces explosive products [1845–1846, Schönbein] — external/team-discoverable (and the direct programmatic precursor)
- Recognition that glycerol is a trihydric alcohol bearing three hydroxylatable positions, making it a candidate for exhaustive nitration [implicit in Chevreul's fat chemistry, ~1823] — external/team-discoverable
- Empirical observation that nitrated organic compounds can be both chemically unstable and physiologically active [1840s, accumulating lab experience] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint is the emergence of mixed-acid nitration as a recognized laboratory technique and the programmatic framing that followed guncotton. All material precursors — glycerol, concentrated nitric acid, concentrated sulfuric acid, ice-bath temperature control — were available by the 1830s, and a motivated team could have assembled them. However, the conceptual trigger that made glycerol an obvious candidate was the guncotton work of 1845–46, which established that exhaustive nitration of polyfunctional organic molecules yields powerfully energetic products; without that framing, a team working in 1838–1842 would have needed to select glycerol from among many organic substrates largely by empirical survey rather than directed reasoning, making success plausible but slower. The serendipity flag is warranted but only partially: the synthesis of glycerol trinitrate was deliberate and reproducible from first attempt given correct procedure; what was unexpected was the extreme mechanical shock-sensitivity and physiological potency. This does not block earliest-plausible dating because the product would have been made and noted regardless of whether its explosive character was anticipated — the accidental detonation Sobrero suffered is evidence of discovery, not a prerequisite for it. The gap from ~1838 to 1847 is under ten years and requires no special explanation; the invention tracks tightly with the maturation of organic nitration chemistry in the 1840s French-German research environment.

full_text:

Year actually invented: 1848

Earliest straightforward: 1820–1835
Confidence: High
Actual location: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (England/Scotland), France, USA (eastern seaboard)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in the USA, which by 1848 had mature steam infrastructure and urgent demand from railway/tunnel construction; the earliest plausible window centers on Britain and France, which led in steam-power engineering and had comparable hard-rock tunneling demands (canal and early railway works) a generation earlier.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Atmospheric/steam engine with reciprocating piston [Newcomen 1712; Watt 1769] — external
- High-pressure steam boiler and pump (capable of compressing air as well as raising water) [Trevithick c.1800] — external
- Air receiver / pressure vessel with safety valve [late 18th-century pneumatic chemistry apparatus; practical boiler technology by 1800] — external/team-reachable
- Reciprocating piston-and-cylinder mechanism [Watt/Boulton engines, widely known by 1790] — external
- Rotary or slide valve for alternating fluid admission [Watt's D-slide valve c.1800; earlier stopcock valves] — external
- Hand-held rock drill and chisel (manual spring-pole or jumper drill) [ancient; well-developed by 1700] — external
- Wrought-iron / cast-iron pipe and fittings for compressed-air distribution [Wilkinson boring mill 1774; standard by 1800] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Boyle's Law and compression of air [Boyle 1662] — external
- Atmospheric pressure and vacuum experiments [Guericke 1650s; Boyle/Hooke 1660s] — external
- Empirical observation that compressed air retains energy and can perform mechanical work at a distance [pneumatic tools and blowing engines, 18th century] — external
- Understanding that a reciprocating air-driven piston can deliver percussive blows to a drill steel [direct analogy to steam hammer concept; Nasmyth steam hammer 1839, but percussive piston logic known earlier from pump and engine work] — team-discoverable by ~1800
Explanation: The binding item is the availability of reliable high-pressure compressed-air supply, which depends on Trevithick-era high-pressure boiler and pump technology (c.1800). All other elements — the reciprocating piston, the valve gear, the chisel, the pressure vessel, and the conceptual understanding of compressed air doing work — were in place or directly team-reachable by roughly 1800–1810. A motivated engineer-artisan team aware of Watt's engine, Trevithick's high-pressure work, and the practical need to speed up hard-rock drilling for canal or early railway tunnels could have assembled a pneumatic percussive drill without any new science; the combination is a straightforward mechanical synthesis. The ~40-year gap between the earliest plausible date and Couch's 1848 patent reflects the absence of pressing economic demand (large-scale hard-rock railway tunneling only became urgent in the 1840s) rather than any technical barrier, placing this invention firmly in the demand-pull rather than knowledge-constrained category.

full_text:
Invention: Pin-tumbler cylinder lock
Year actually invented: 1848
Earliest plausible: 1780–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1840
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Newport, New York, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (London/Birmingham), France (Paris), Germany (Nuremberg/Solingen)
Geographic note: MISMATCH. The earliest plausible date points to Britain or continental Europe, where precision metal-working, industrial filing, and the organized locksmithing trade were most advanced in the late 18th century; Yale Sr.'s American realization came a generation after these capabilities were fully available in European craft centers.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Precision metal boring and reaming of cylindrical housings [~1770s, with Wilkinson-era boring technology] — external/team-reachable
- Small coil or leaf spring manufacture in metal [well established by ~1700] — external/team-reachable
- Drawn brass or steel rod stock in consistent small diameters [~1720–1750] — external/team-reachable
- File-cut key blanks with profiled bitting [established locksmithing practice, ~1700s] — external/team-reachable
- Rotating-cylinder lock housing concept (Bramah, 1784) [1784] — external/team-reachable
- Precision gauge-and-tolerance fitting for lock components (Bramah/Maudslay tradition, ~1790s) [~1795] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Shear-line principle: that a rotating plug can be blocked by pins bridging housing and plug, and released when all pin interfaces align at one radius [empirically obvious from wooden Egyptian locks, transferable without new theory] — external/team-discoverable
- Spring-force calibration sufficient to return pins without jamming the plug [empirical iteration, no new science] — external/team-discoverable
- Tolerances required for reliable shear-line alignment in metal (~0.005–0.01 inch) — achievable by skilled filer/turner once Maudslay-tradition precision work was established [~1800] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding item is precision metal manufacture at the scale of small cylindrical components. The ancient Egyptian wooden pin lock demonstrated the operating principle as early as ~2000 BCE, and the principle was never lost or forgotten — it was simply impractical to replicate reliably in metal until boring machines and precision hand-fitting reached sufficient accuracy. Iron and brass locks existed throughout the medieval period, but hand-filing could not hold tolerances tight enough for multiple spring-loaded metal pins to all reach a common shear line without either jamming or offering no meaningful security. The gap of roughly 3,600 years is therefore not a failure of imagination but a materials-and-manufacturing gap: craftsmen working before the Bramah–Maudslay precision-machining tradition (~1784–1800) simply could not guarantee that five or six bored pin chambers in a metal cylinder would be co-axial within the required fraction of a millimeter, nor that drawn-wire springs and turned pin blanks would be consistent enough for reliable operation. Once Bramah patented his rotating-cylinder lock in 1784 and Maudslay's slide-rest lathe and surface-plate techniques became available by roughly 1800, a motivated team in a well-equipped London or Birmingham workshop had every component needed to build Yale Sr.'s exact design; the earliest plausible window is therefore 1780–1800, with straightforward execution more likely 1820–1840 as precision small-parts manufacture diffused beyond a handful of elite shops.
