full_text:
Invention: Metallic-cartridge repeating rifle
Year actually invented: 1849
Earliest plausible: 1845–1850
Earliest straightforward: 1855–1865
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: New York, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: USA, France, Belgium, United Kingdom
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The USA and Western Europe (particularly France and Belgium) had the precision metalworking, firearms craft traditions, and percussion ignition knowledge necessary; Hunt's New York location was plausible, and the parallel European pinfire work confirms the geography was broadly correct.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Percussion cap ignition [1820s, Shaw/Forsyth] — external/team-reachable
- Tubular magazine lever-action repeating mechanism (Kalthoff repeating musket concept) [c.1640–1660] — external/team-reachable
- Drawn or deep-pressed metal cup/case fabrication (copper drawing for percussion caps) [1820s–1830s] — external/team-reachable
- Lefaucheux pinfire cartridge (self-contained powder+ball+primer in case) [1836–1846] — external/team-reachable
- Flobert BB cap (primer-only self-contained metallic cartridge) [1845] — external/team-reachable
- Precision rifled barrel and chamber fitting [1840s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Copper's malleability and gas-sealing properties under chamber pressure (empirical, no new science needed) [1830s–1840s] — external/team-discoverable
- Ballistic benefit of conical hollow-base bullet (Minié ball concept) [1823–1849] — external/team-discoverable
- Percussion-sensitive priming compounds (fulminate of mercury) [1800s, Howard] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint for a true metallic-cartridge repeating rifle is the self-contained metallic cartridge that seals propellant gases and carries its own primer in a drawn metal case — without it, a repeating mechanism cannot reliably prevent chain-fire or gas blowback at the breech. The Lefaucheux pinfire (1836–1846) and Flobert BB cap (1845) establish that by the mid-1840s the conceptual and manufacturing groundwork for a self-contained metallic cartridge existed in Western Europe, making Hunt's 1849 Rocket Ball solution (a functional if crude analogue) plausible at essentially the same moment. A motivated team with access to copper-drawing technology, percussion chemistry, and existing repeating-mechanism patents could have arrived at a workable design in the 1845–1850 window; however, producing a truly reliable, gas-tight rimfire or pinfire repeating rifle at scale required iterating on case dimensions, chamber tolerances, and extraction — pushing the straightforward date to 1855–1865, when S&W .22 rimfire (1857) and Henry rifle (1860) achieved it commercially. The gap between Hunt's 1849 design and the earliest plausible date is negligible; the gap to a fully reliable metallic-cartridge repeater is roughly a decade, explained by the engineering challenge of consistent case drawing and extraction rather than any missing scientific knowledge.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Switzerland; USA (New England mill districts)
geographic_note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in a major industrial mill district (Lowell, MA) with strong hydraulic engineering culture; the earliest plausible date would similarly favor Britain and France, where precision metalworking, hydraulic millwork, and systematic engineering practice were most concentrated by 1800–1810.
full_text:
Invention: Francis water turbine
Year actually invented: 1849
Earliest plausible: 1800–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1830–1840
Confidence: High
Actual location: Lowell, Massachusetts, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Switzerland, USA (New England mill districts)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in a major industrial mill district (Lowell, MA) with strong hydraulic engineering culture; the earliest plausible date would similarly favor Britain and France, where precision metalworking, hydraulic millwork, and systematic engineering practice were most concentrated by 1800–1810.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Cast and wrought iron precision casting and machining (curved vane profiles) [~1780s onward] — external/team-reachable
- Spiral/volute casing fabrication in metal [~1780s onward] — external/team-reachable
- Fourneyron outward-flow reaction turbine concept (demonstrates full-reaction principle in practice) [1827] — external; absent before ~1827, so blocks the straightforward path before that date
- Guide vane (wicket gate) geometry for flow regulation [~1820s–1830s] — external/team-reachable
- Precision hydraulic model testing apparatus (scale models, head/flow measurement) [~1790s onward] — team-reachable via empirical iteration
- Jonval/axial-flow turbine (demonstrates mixed-flow concept possibilities) [1837] — external; useful but not strictly binding
- Water wheel and millwork tradition (empirical hydraulic engineering base) [ancient, well-developed by 1750] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Bernoulli's theorem (pressure-velocity energy exchange in flowing fluid) [1738] — external
- Euler's turbomachinery equation (relating blade geometry, flow velocity, and torque) [1754] — external; critically, this means the mathematical framework for optimizing runner blade angles predates 1849 by nearly a century and is fully citable
- Continuity equation and hydraulic head concepts (Torricelli, 1640s; formalized by 1750) — external
- Empirical understanding of draft-tube pressure recovery [team-discoverable by ~1800 via iteration]
Explanation: The binding item for the earliest plausible date (~1800–1810) is the availability of Euler's turbomachinery equations (1754) and Bernoulli's theorem (1738) as external knowledge, combined with the precision metal-casting capability present in Britain and France by the 1790s; a motivated engineer-artisan team could have empirically iterated toward an inward mixed-flow reaction runner using scale-model hydraulic testing without requiring any new scientific framework. The reason the straightforward date is substantially later (~1830–1840) is that Fourneyron's 1827 turbine provided the decisive existence proof that a full-reaction turbine dramatically outperformed impulse wheels — without that demonstrated concept, the team would need to discover the reaction principle empirically, which is possible but far less directed; after 1827 (and especially after the Henschel-Jonval axial variant of the late 1830s), the design space narrows sufficiently that systematic blade-profile iteration guided by Euler's equations makes the Francis geometry a near-inevitable engineering outcome within the five-year team window.

possible_locations: England; France; Netherlands; Germany
geographic_note: MISMATCH. The actual invention occurred in the United States, but the prerequisite industrial wire-drawing and spring-tempering capabilities were concentrated in European metalworking centers (particularly England and the German states) significantly earlier; the invention could plausibly have emerged there well before the American context that produced Hunt.
full_text:
Invention: Safety pin
Year actually invented: 1849
Earliest plausible: 1650–1700
Earliest straightforward: 1700–1750
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: New York, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Netherlands, Germany
Geographic note: MISMATCH. The actual invention occurred in the United States, but the prerequisite industrial wire-drawing and spring-tempering capabilities were concentrated in European metalworking centers (particularly England and the German states) significantly earlier; the invention could plausibly have emerged there well before the American context that produced Hunt.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Wire drawing (iron/steel, consistent gauge) [c. 1350–1400, Germany/Nuremberg] — external/team-reachable
- Spring tempering of steel wire (coil springs, clock springs) [c. 1550–1600] — external/team-reachable
- Fibula/brooch tradition (pin-and-catch geometry, spring-bow concept) [c. 1400 BCE, continuous tradition] — external/team-reachable
- Stamped or formed metal clasp/guard manufacturing [c. 1600–1650] — external/team-reachable
- Thin, consistent steel wire suitable for cold-forming into coil spring [c. 1620–1650, England/Netherlands] — external/team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Elastic spring behavior of tempered steel (empirical, no theory required) [c. 1550–1600] — external/team-discoverable
- Work-hardening and tempering of wire to hold set geometry (empirical metalworking knowledge) [c. 1600] — external/team-discoverable
- Geometry of coil spring providing axial tension sufficient to hold clasp closed (empirical, one iteration) [team-discoverable within project scope] — external/team-discoverable

Explanation: The binding item is the combination of fine spring-tempered steel wire of consistent gauge with the specific geometric insight of integrating the protective clasp — the latter being conceptually trivial once the fibula tradition and spring-wire technology are both in hand. By approximately 1650–1700, European wire-drawing and spring-tempering were sufficiently mature that a motivated artisan team could have produced Hunt's exact design empirically within the five-year window; the coil-spring tension and point-guard clasp require no new scientific knowledge, only the deliberate combination of elements from clock-spring practice and the ancient fibula tradition. The gap between earliest plausible (~1650–1700) and actual invention (1849) is roughly 150–200 years and warrants a Flag and historical explanation: the fibula had never disappeared from European culture, yet the full safety pin was not made because the fibula tradition satisfied the functional need adequately and no strong commercial pressure existed to improve it; the needle and pin trades were highly specialized and guild-structured, which discouraged recombinant innovation across craft boundaries; and the protective clasp — the key differentiating element — was only an obvious improvement in a context where mass-produced, sharply-pointed machine-drawn pins posed a specific handling hazard at scale, a condition that did not arise until cheap industrial pin manufacture (post-1820s) made loose sharp pins ubiquitous. Hunt himself invented the safety pin in three hours specifically to pay off a debt, suggesting the combination was obvious once attended to, reinforcing that institutional and economic inattention — not technical barriers — explain the entire gap.

possible_locations: England (London; Birmingham; Manchester); France (Paris); Netherlands
geographic_note: ALIGNED. England was the center of industrial hydraulics, with Bramah's press (1795) and a mature millwright/engineering culture; Armstrong himself worked in Newcastle, the heart of heavy industrial engineering.
full_text:
Invention: Hydraulic accumulator
Year actually invented: 1850
Earliest plausible: 1800–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1810–1820
Confidence: High
Actual location: Newcastle upon Tyne, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England (London, Birmingham, Manchester), France (Paris), Netherlands
Geographic note: ALIGNED. England was the center of industrial hydraulics, with Bramah's press (1795) and a mature millwright/engineering culture; Armstrong himself worked in Newcastle, the heart of heavy industrial engineering.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Pascal's law / hydrostatic pressure transmission [1648] — external/team-reachable
- Bramah hydraulic press (high-pressure cylinder, water-tight leather cup seals) [1795] — external/team-reachable
- Cast-iron cylinder boring (Wilkinson boring mill) [1775] — external/team-reachable
- Wrought-iron and cast-iron heavy structural fabrication [~1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Leather and hemp packing seals for hydraulic cylinders [pre-1795] — external/team-reachable
- Hydraulic pump technology (force pumps) [ancient, refined by ~1700] — external/team-reachable
- Connecting pipework in cast iron [~1790s] — external/team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Pascal's principle: pressure applied to enclosed fluid transmits equally in all directions [1648] — external/team-discoverable
- Conservation of energy / gravitational potential energy (empirical understanding sufficient; formal statement not required) [empirical, pre-1800] — external/team-discoverable
- Empirical observation that a weighted piston can store and release fluid energy predictably — team-discoverable by direct experiment

Explanation: The hydraulic accumulator requires no scientific insight beyond Pascal's law (1648) and the intuitive understanding that a raised weight stores energy. The sole binding prerequisite is Bramah's hydraulic press (1795), which established high-pressure cylinder-and-seal technology capable of operating at the pressures Armstrong's accumulator demands; without reliable high-pressure seals and precision-bored cylinders, a leaking or bursting accumulator is not a usable device. All other elements — cast-iron cylinders, force pumps, heavy ram fabrication, connecting pipes — were standard millwright practice by 1800. A motivated engineer in England ca. 1800–1810 could have assembled these components empirically within five years, making 1800–1810 the earliest plausible window; the concept is so mechanically direct (a weighted piston on a hydraulic cylinder) that once high-pressure hydraulics existed, invention was nearly inevitable, hence high confidence and no flag.

possible_locations: London; UK; Paris; France; Amsterdam; Netherlands
geographic_note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in London, which was also the most plausible early location given its combination of municipal sewer infrastructure, dense urban population creating demand, and prior flush closet development by Cumming and Bramah.
full_text:
Invention: Public flush toilet
Year actually invented: 1851
Earliest plausible: 1780–1790
Earliest straightforward: 1800–1820
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: London, United Kingdom
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: London, UK; Paris, France; Amsterdam, Netherlands
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in London, which was also the most plausible early location given its combination of municipal sewer infrastructure, dense urban population creating demand, and prior flush closet development by Cumming and Bramah.

Prerequisite technologies:
- S-trap / water-sealed trap (Cumming's patent) [1775] — external
- Improved flap-valve flush closet (Bramah) [1778] — external
- Overhead cistern with release valve mechanism [1778] — external
- Cast-iron or lead supply pipes capable of pressurized delivery [~1750s] — external
- Brick-lined sewer or drainage channel connected to watercourse [~1700s, London] — external
- Coin-operated locking mechanism (simple turnstile or coin-drop bolt) [~1750s, mechanical locksmithing] — team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Miasma theory (sewer gases cause disease) — sufficient motivation for gas-trapping seals [widely held by 1750] — external
- Empirical observation that water seal blocks odor transmission through pipe [demonstrable by 1775] — external
- Hydraulic understanding that gravity-fed cistern provides adequate flush volume [empirical, no theory required] — external

Explanation: By 1780 all mechanical components existed: Bramah's flush closet (1778) provided a reliable flush mechanism and water-sealed trap, cast-iron pipe supply was available, and London already had rudimentary brick sewers draining to the Thames. The sole binding item is not technology but institutional will — a property owner, municipality, or entrepreneur willing to install a shared flush closet in a public or commercial space and manage water supply and drainage for multiple strangers. A motivated team in 1780 London could have sourced Bramah closets off the shelf, plumbed them to an existing sewer, fitted a simple coin-drop bolt (well within contemporary locksmithing), and charged a penny per use. The gap to 1851 (~70 years) is therefore social and economic rather than technical: urban water supply was intermittent and metered, sewer connections were privately negotiated, sanitary reform was not yet a political priority, and no clear revenue model had been demonstrated for public conveniences. Jennings succeeded partly because the Great Exhibition provided a captive high-density audience that proved the commercial case, and because 1840s sewer expansion in London finally made reliable drainage connections routine.

possible_locations: United Kingdom (London; Edinburgh); France (Paris); Germany (Berlin; Göttingen); Sweden (Stockholm)
geographic_note: ALIGNED. Germany was a leading center of analytical and inorganic chemistry by mid-century, and Bunsen's Heidelberg laboratory was well-resourced; the technique required no infrastructure unavailable elsewhere in Western Europe.
full_text:
Invention: Chemical-vapor deposition
Year actually invented: 1852
Earliest plausible: 1800–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1830–1840
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Heidelberg, Germany
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (London, Edinburgh), France (Paris), Germany (Berlin, Göttingen), Sweden (Stockholm)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Germany was a leading center of analytical and inorganic chemistry by mid-century, and Bunsen's Heidelberg laboratory was well-resourced; the technique required no infrastructure unavailable elsewhere in Western Europe.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Tube furnace / high-temperature glass or porcelain tube apparatus [~1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Hydrogen gas generation via zinc + acid (Cavendish) [1766] — external/team-reachable
- Synthesis and handling of volatile metal chlorides (AsCl3, SnCl4, TiCl4) [1790s–1820s] — external/team-reachable
- Bellows or controlled gas-flow plumbing for directing vapor streams [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Clean metal or ceramic substrates for deposition [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Lavoisier's oxygen theory and systematic inorganic chemistry [1780s–1790s] — external/team-discoverable
- Observation that metal halides decompose or are reduced at elevated temperatures (empirical, no new theory required) [1800s, incremental] — external/team-discoverable
- Hydrogen as a reducing agent for metal oxides and halides (Gay-Lussac, Davy) [1800–1810] — external/team-discoverable
- Volatile metal halide chemistry (Klaproth, Vauquelin, others) [1790s–1820s] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding limiting factor is not any single piece of apparatus or theory but the combination of: (1) reliable synthesis and safe handling of volatile, corrosive metal chlorides such as CrCl3 and TiCl4, which required systematic inorganic halide chemistry that crystallized only in the late 1790s–1820s, and (2) the conceptual framing of deliberately using gas-phase reduction as a deposition technique rather than merely observing incidental metal films as curiosities. A motivated team working in the Lavoisier tradition with access to Davy's electrochemical and reducing-gas work (post-1807) could, in principle, have combined zinc-acid hydrogen generation, a heated porcelain tube, and a known volatile chloride to produce deposited metal films by roughly 1800–1810; the earliest straightforward date is pushed to 1830–1840 because reliable preparative routes to CrCl3 and TiCl4 and safe glass-blowing for closed tube systems were only routine by then, and without a clear purpose (thin-film optics, corrosion protection) the experiment would have been unlikely to be attempted deliberately rather than stumbled upon.

possible_locations: United Kingdom (England); France; Germany; United States
geographic_note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in an industrializing American manufacturing center; the earliest plausible locations are the leading industrialized nations of the late 18th–early 19th century, all of which possessed the requisite metalworking, spring manufacture, and mine-hoist infrastructure.
full_text:
Invention: Safety-brake elevator
Year actually invented: 1852
Earliest plausible: 1790–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1840
Confidence: High
Actual location: Yonkers, New York, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (England), France, Germany, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in an industrializing American manufacturing center; the earliest plausible locations are the leading industrialized nations of the late 18th–early 19th century, all of which possessed the requisite metalworking, spring manufacture, and mine-hoist infrastructure.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Toothed/ratcheted guide rails (mine cage guides) [~1750s] — external/team-reachable
- Leaf and coil spring manufacture (carriage and clockmaking industry) [~1700s] — external/team-reachable
- Pawl-and-ratchet mechanisms (clocks, windlasses, capstans) [ancient, well-developed by ~1600] — external/team-reachable
- Steam-powered winding/hoisting machinery (mine headframes) [~1780s, Watt improvements] — external/team-reachable
- Wrought-iron and cast-iron structural components (beams, brackets) [~1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Guide-rail enclosed cage hoists for mines [~1750s–1770s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Mechanics of spring compression and release (Hooke's Law) [1678] — external/team-discoverable
- Friction and normal-force braking sufficient to arrest a loaded cage (empirical, no novel theory required) [understood by ~1750] — external/team-discoverable
- Load dynamics of falling vs. stationary mass on a ratchet (empirical shop knowledge) [~1750s–1780s] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: Every component of the Otis safety elevator — toothed guide rails, enclosed cage, hoisting rope, and spring-loaded pawl that engages automatically on rope slack — existed in fully developed form within the British and European mining industry by the 1780s–1800s. Watt-era steam winding engines drove enclosed mine cages along timber guides; ratchet and pawl mechanisms were standard millwright and clockmaker knowledge; calibrated leaf springs were produced commercially for carriages. The binding insight is purely combinatorial: mounting a spring-loaded outward-engaging ratchet catch on the cage itself, pre-loaded by rope tension and released by rope failure. No new scientific framework, no novel material, and no serendipitous discovery is required — only a motivated engineer who perceived the safety problem (rope-break fatality in a man-carrying cage) and applied existing ratchet-spring technology to solve it. A competent millwright-and-springmaker team working in an industrialized setting circa 1790–1810 could have prototyped and validated this mechanism empirically within five years. The ~60-year gap between earliest plausible and actual invention reflects the limited commercial demand for safe passenger vertical transport before urban multi-story building density created the economic incentive that Otis's era finally supplied.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Germany
geographic_note: ALIGNED. France and Britain were the leading centers of both aeronautics (ballooning tradition since 1783) and lightweight steam engine development (Trevithick, high-pressure engines by 1800–1810), making either country a plausible site for an earlier attempt.
full_text:
Invention: Powered dirigible flight
Year actually invented: 1852
Earliest plausible: 1820–1830
Earliest straightforward: 1835–1845
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Germany
Geographic note: ALIGNED. France and Britain were the leading centers of both aeronautics (ballooning tradition since 1783) and lightweight steam engine development (Trevithick, high-pressure engines by 1800–1810), making either country a plausible site for an earlier attempt.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Gas envelope / free balloon (hydrogen or coal gas) [1783] — external
- Montgolfier hot-air balloon (proof of concept for large lift envelopes) [1783] — external
- Cigar/elongated envelope shape for aerodynamic steering [understood geometrically by 1800s, no new science required] — team-reachable
- Trevithick high-pressure lightweight steam engine [1800–1804] — external
- Screw propeller / paddle propulsion applied to water craft [1790s–1810s] — external
- Rope/chain transmission from engine to propeller shaft [extant by 1810] — external
- Rudder and control car suspended below envelope [direct extension of balloon car practice, 1783] — team-reachable
- Boiler miniaturization sufficient to reduce deadweight [iterative; achievable 1815–1825 with Trevithick lineage] — team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Buoyancy / Archimedes principle (lift from displaced air) [antiquity, confirmed experimentally by 1783] — external
- Hydrogen production via acid-on-iron [Cavendish 1766] — external
- Coal gas production and storage [Murdoch 1790s–1802] — external
- Drag increases with cross-sectional area and velocity squared (empirical, known to balloon experimenters by 1800s) — team-discoverable
- Power-to-weight ratio of steam engines improving on Trevithick high-pressure designs [empirical, 1804–1820] — external/team-discoverable
- Propeller thrust sufficient to overcome envelope drag at low speed (empirical iteration, no new theory needed) — team-discoverable

Explanation: The binding constraint is the power-to-weight ratio of available steam engines relative to the aerodynamic drag of a large gas envelope. Watt-era low-pressure engines (pre-1800) were far too heavy; a Watt engine of sufficient horsepower would consume nearly all available lift. Trevithick's high-pressure compact engines (1800–1804) broke this constraint in principle, and by roughly 1815–1820 iterative refinement of high-pressure boiler and engine design had produced units light enough that a motivated team could plausibly have achieved a marginal power surplus for slow controlled flight. The one-layer precursor allowance covers boiler miniaturization as a direct engineering derivative of Trevithick's work. Coal gas as a lifting medium (cheaper than hydrogen) was available from Murdoch's gasworks by the early 1800s. Envelope and car construction were straightforward extensions of established balloon practice. A skilled engineering team in Britain or France with access to a Trevithick-lineage engine and a hydrogen or coal-gas balloon could plausibly have assembled a steerable dirigible by the mid-1820s, with a straightforward (non-heroic) effort achievable by the late 1830s; Giffard's 1852 date therefore reflects modest historical lag rather than any fundamental knowledge gap.

possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Belgium
geographic_note: ALIGNED. Britain and France led in both iron construction and cement chemistry in this period; the actual invention in Paris fits the broader northwest European industrial core where all prerequisite materials and structural knowledge converged.
full_text:
Invention: Reinforced concrete
Year actually invented: 1853
Earliest plausible: 1790–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1824–1835
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Belgium
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Britain and France led in both iron construction and cement chemistry in this period; the actual invention in Paris fits the broader northwest European industrial core where all prerequisite materials and structural knowledge converged.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Wrought iron structural members (beams, rods, columns) [~1790] — external/team-reachable
- Hydraulic lime / natural hydraulic cement (e.g., Roman cement, Parker's cement) [~1796] — external/team-reachable
- Pozzolanic and hydraulic binder knowledge (empirical, from Roman and post-Renaissance practice) [pre-1750] — external/team-reachable
- Portland cement (Aspdin patent) [1824] — external/team-reachable
- Formwork and casting techniques for mass concrete [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Wrought iron wire and rod drawing at commercial scale [~1820s] — external/team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical observation that concrete and masonry fail in tension before compression [widely known by ~1750] — external/team-discoverable
- Qualitative understanding of composite action (iron carries tension, masonry/concrete carries compression) [inferable by ~1800 from bridge and floor practice] — external/team-discoverable
- No formal elastic theory required; the combination can be reached empirically by a builder observing cracking patterns — external/team-discoverable
- Coulomb's masonry rupture analysis (qualitative tension/compression distinction) [1773] — external/team-discoverable

Explanation: The binding item before ~1790 is the absence of commercially available iron rod at meaningful scale combined with the absence of reliable hydraulic cement — without both, the composite cannot be formed as a practical structural element rather than a curiosity. Once wrought iron rod production became routine in Britain and France (~1790) and Parker's Roman cement (1796) or equivalent natural hydraulic cements were available, a motivated builder-engineer team could have arrived at the combination empirically: observe that plain lime-concrete slabs crack on the underside under load, embed iron rods on the tension face, test and iterate. Portland cement (1824) makes the matrix stronger and more consistent, pushing the "straightforward" window to 1824–1835, but it is not strictly required. The 53-year gap between the earliest plausible date (~1800) and the actual invention (1853) is historically explained by the dominance of traditional masonry and timber construction, a craft culture that treated iron and concrete as independent systems, the absence of any theoretical framework motivating the combination, and the lack of economic pressure until mid-century urban construction demand intensified — not by any missing material or scientific knowledge.

possible_locations: UK (London; Edinburgh); Germany (Berlin; Leipzig); France (Paris)
geographic_note: ALIGNED. The UK and Germany were the leading centers of photographic chemistry and color theory in this period; Maxwell's actual location is fully consistent with where earliest plausible work would have occurred.
full_text:
Invention: Practical three-color photography
Year actually invented: 1855
Earliest plausible: 1873–1878
Earliest straightforward: 1878–1885
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: Edinburgh / London, UK
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: UK (London, Edinburgh), Germany (Berlin, Leipzig), France (Paris)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The UK and Germany were the leading centers of photographic chemistry and color theory in this period; Maxwell's actual location is fully consistent with where earliest plausible work would have occurred.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Silver-halide wet collodion photographic plates [1851, Frederick Scott Archer] — external/team-reachable
- Dry gelatin silver bromide plates [1871, Richard Leach Maddox; practical by 1878] — external/team-reachable
- Dye sensitization of silver-halide emulsions (orthochromatic sensitizers: eosin/erythrosine) [1873, Hermann Wilhelm Vogel] — external/team-reachable
- Panchromatic dye sensitization (red-sensitive emulsions) [1873–1884, Vogel and successors] — external/team-reachable
- Precision colored glass and gelatin optical filters [c. 1850s, widely available] — external/team-reachable
- Optical projection lanterns with adequate arc-lamp or limelight illumination [1850s–1860s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Young-Helmholtz trichromatic color vision theory (three-receptor hypothesis) [Young 1802; Helmholtz 1850–1867] — external/team-discoverable
- Maxwell's color-mixing experiments and spinning-disk colorimetry establishing RGB primaries [1855–1861] — external/team-discoverable
- Empirical observation that unsensitized silver emulsions respond only to blue/violet/UV, not red or green [well-known by 1860s] — external/team-discoverable
- Vogel's discovery that dyes adsorbed onto silver grains extend spectral sensitivity [1873] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: Maxwell's 1861 demonstration is a Flag case because the mechanism that made it work was serendipitous: his nominally "red" filter passed UV radiation, to which unsensitized silver-halide plates are sensitive, rather than red light; without this accident the red-channel plate would have been nearly blank. A directed engineering team — aware from the outset that standard plates are blind to red and green — would not have replicated the UV-leakage trick deliberately; instead they would have pursued the correct route of spectral sensitization. That route became available only after Vogel's 1873 discovery of dye sensitization, which for the first time allowed emulsions to record green and, eventually, red light. The binding item is therefore Vogel's dye-sensitization work (1873), not the trichromatic theory or the filter/projection hardware, all of which were in place by the mid-1850s. Given the five-year horizon and the need for one layer of precursor development (improving panchromatic dye sensitivity enough to record deep red), the earliest plausible window is 1873–1878; the straightforward window, when reliably red-sensitive panchromatic plates were practically achievable, is 1878–1885. The actual location (UK/Germany) is fully aligned with where Vogel's photochemistry and Maxwell's color theory intersected.

possible_locations: England; Belgium; France; Sweden; United States (Pennsylvania)
geographic_note: ALIGNED. England was the global center of iron and steel production, blast furnace technology, and industrial empirical engineering in the early 19th century; the invention naturally emerged there and could plausibly have emerged in no more than a handful of other leading ironworking nations.
full_text:
Invention: Bessemer Steel Process
Year actually invented: 1855
Earliest plausible: 1820–1835
Earliest straightforward: 1835–1850
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: London, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, Belgium, France, Sweden, United States (Pennsylvania)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. England was the global center of iron and steel production, blast furnace technology, and industrial empirical engineering in the early 19th century; the invention naturally emerged there and could plausibly have emerged in no more than a handful of other leading ironworking nations.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Blast furnace with forced-air blowing engines [~1700s, mature by 1800] — external/team-reachable
- Refractory brick linings (silica/fireclay) capable of withstanding molten iron temperatures [~1780–1810] — external/team-reachable
- Tiltable/tappable vessel designs for handling molten iron [~1800–1820] — external/team-reachable
- Hot-blast iron smelting (Neilson patent) [1828] — external; improves pig iron quality and availability but not strictly required for the converter concept
- Mechanical blowers / steam-powered air pumps [~1790–1810] — external/team-reachable

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Empirical understanding that carbon content governs iron hardness and steel properties (wrought < 0.1% C; steel 0.1–1.5% C; cast iron ~2–4% C) [well-established by ~1800 among practical ironmasters, though not yet fully theorized] — external/team-discoverable
- Observation that combustion of carbon and silicon in iron is strongly exothermic — sufficient to maintain or raise melt temperature [empirically discoverable; not requiring formal thermochemistry] — team-discoverable by iterative trial
- Lavoisier's oxygen chemistry and combustion theory [1770s–1780s] — external; provides conceptual framing but the process can be developed purely empirically without it
- Knowledge that wrought iron is made by oxidizing ("puddling") pig iron — puddling process (Cort, 1784) demonstrates air/oxygen removes carbon [external, well-known by 1800]

Explanation: The binding constraint is not scientific knowledge but a specific non-obvious empirical insight: that forcing a cold air blast through a large volume of molten pig iron will not freeze the melt but will instead superheat it via exothermic combustion of carbon and silicon, sustaining and accelerating the reaction. Every other component — the vessel, the blowers, the refractory lining, the empirical carbon-content lore, and the analogy from puddling — was available by roughly 1800–1820. The gap to 1855 is therefore primarily one of conceptual imagination: no ironmaster had combined forced-air blasting (used at the tuyere level in blast furnaces, below the melt) with a converter vessel holding a full charge of molten iron. A motivated team after ~1820 could have built a prototype converter, observed the exothermic blow, and iterated toward a working process within five years. The earliest plausible date is pushed to 1820–1835 rather than pre-1820 mainly because reliable steam-powered blowers of sufficient capacity and good-quality pig iron in quantity were not consistently available before roughly 1810–1820. The semi-accidental nature of Bessemer's original observation is flagged but does not dominate the assessment, since the underlying mechanism is empirically discoverable by any team systematically exploring air-blast effects on molten iron; the process did not require the accident, only the question.

full_text:
Invention: Parkesine (celluloid)
Year actually invented: 1856
Earliest plausible: 1846–1850
Earliest straightforward: 1848–1852
Confidence: High
Actual location: Birmingham, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, Germany, France, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Birmingham was a center of industrial chemistry and skilled artisan manufacturing; the actual inventor worked precisely in the environment most capable of producing this invention at the earliest plausible date.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Nitrocellulose (guncotton) synthesis [1845, Schönbein] — external
- Camphor production and availability as a refined chemical reagent [well before 1845] — external
- Ethanol and ether as industrial solvents [well before 1845] — external
- Solvent-casting and lacquer-coating techniques (e.g., collodion work) [1846–1848] — external/team-reachable
- Basic thermoplastic molding concepts (e.g., shellac molding, gutta-percha processing) [1840s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Nitrocellulose dissolves in mixed ether-ethanol solvents to form collodion [1846–1847, Maynard/Schönbein] — external
- Camphor as a softening/plasticizing agent for resins observed empirically [pre-1845] — team-discoverable by systematic trial
- Observation that camphor-loaded nitrocellulose films retain flexibility and can be compression-molded when warm [team-discoverable by 1847–1848]
Explanation: The binding prerequisite is nitrocellulose, first synthesized in 1845. With nitrocellulose available by early 1846 and collodion (dissolved nitrocellulose) techniques spreading rapidly through the photographic and medical communities by 1847, a motivated Birmingham workshop with access to published chemical literature and standard reagents including camphor, ethanol, and ether could have empirically explored plasticizer additives within one to two years. Camphor's resin-softening properties were known from lacquer and varnish practice, and no new scientific framework is required — only systematic mixing, casting, and compression trials. The team-reachable layer of precursor technology (collodion handling) was itself a direct downstream product of the 1845 discovery, placing the earliest plausible window at 1846–1850; the straightforward window of 1848–1852 accounts for the time needed for nitrocellulose synthesis knowledge to diffuse to industrial chemists and for camphor as a specific plasticizer to be identified by trial among candidate additives.

full_text:
Invention: Vapour-compression ice machine
Year actually invented: 1856
Earliest plausible: 1834–1840
Earliest straightforward: 1840–1848
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Geelong, Australia / Glasgow, Scotland
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom (London, Glasgow, Birmingham), France (Paris), United States (Philadelphia, New York)
Geographic note: MISMATCH. The earliest plausible date precedes Harrison's involvement; the most capable workshops and engineering talent in 1834–1840 were concentrated in industrial Britain and France, not colonial Australia.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Double-acting steam engine / reciprocating compressor [~1800] — external/team-reachable
- Precision metal cylinder boring and piston fitting (Wilkinson boring mill lineage) [~1775–1800] — external/team-reachable
- Copper or wrought-iron tube coil fabrication for heat exchangers [~1820s] — external/team-reachable
- Globe valves and needle valves capable of throttling volatile fluids under pressure [~1820s] — external/team-reachable
- Welded or screwed high-pressure pipe fittings (boiler trade heritage) [~1820s] — external/team-reachable
- Diethyl ether as a working fluid (commercially available, low boiling point well-known) [~1820s] — external/team-reachable
- Brine (salt-water) secondary refrigerant circuit [ancient / trivially known] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Oliver Evans qualitative description of vapor-compression refrigeration cycle [1805] — external/team-discoverable
- Jacob Perkins patent (US 9745X / British 6662) fully specifying compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator cycle with ether [1834] — external/team-discoverable
- Empirical knowledge that volatile liquids (ether, ammonia) absorb large amounts of heat on evaporation [well-established by 1820s] — external/team-discoverable
- Pressure–temperature relationship of ether vapour sufficient for engineering sizing (Dalton, Faraday work) [~1820s] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint was not scientific knowledge — the full thermodynamic cycle was published by Evans (1805) and patented in mechanical detail by Perkins (1834) — but rather practical engineering integration: achieving leak-free, continuous operation under repeated pressure cycling with ether (a highly volatile, fire-prone solvent) required tight piston tolerances, reliable valve timing, and joints that stayed sealed under thermal stress. Perkins himself built a demonstration machine in 1834–1835 that worked but was too unreliable and small-scale for commercial use, indicating the gap to Harrison was one of scale-up engineering and sustained capital investment rather than any missing knowledge layer. A motivated team with access to a first-rate boiler and instrument shop (Birmingham or Glasgow, ~1835–1840) could plausibly have closed the gap within five years of the Perkins patent: the compressor cylinder is directly analogous to a small steam engine, the condenser coil is standard brewery or distillery copper work, and the expansion valve requires only careful needle-valve fitting. The 22-year gap between Perkins (1834) and Harrison's commercial success (1856) is therefore attributed primarily to lack of a clear commercial demand signal, the fire/explosion hazard of ether discouraging industrial investors, and the difficulty of sustaining continuous unattended operation — not to any hard engineering barrier unavailable before 1840.

full_text:
Invention: Mauveine synthetic dye
Year actually invented: 1856
Earliest plausible: 1845–1855
Earliest straightforward: 1850–1858
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: London, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England (London, Manchester), France (Paris, Lyon), Germany (Berlin, Giessen)
Geographic note: ALIGNED. England was the leading center of coal-tar chemistry and textile industry demand; France and Germany had strong organic chemistry traditions that could also have achieved this. Perkin's London location was ideal given coal-tar availability and dye-trade infrastructure.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Isolation of aniline from coal tar (Runge) [1834] — external
- Industrial coal-tar distillation and fractionation [~1820s–1840s] — external
- Potassium dichromate as oxidizing agent in wet chemistry [~1820s] — external
- Ethanol-based dye extraction and mordant dyeing on silk [centuries prior] — external
- Basic organic synthesis glassware and reflux apparatus [~1820s–1830s] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Aniline and toluidine structures (crude aniline contains mixed amines) [1834–1850s] — external; full structural theory not required, empirical use sufficient
- Chromophore behavior of aromatic nitrogen compounds (dye-yielding oxidation products) [empirically discoverable within program]
- Perkin's own observation that coal-tar fractions yield colored oxidation products [1856] — team-discoverable by systematic oxidation screening
- Indigo and natural dye precedents establishing that organic molecules can fix color to fiber [ancient/well-known] — external
Explanation: The binding constraint is not scientific theory but rather the serendipity of the specific oxidation pathway: mauveine arises from crude aniline (a mixture including o- and p-toluidines) reacting with dichromate, and the colored product's value as a textile dye was only recognized because Perkin happened to test the sludge on silk. A directed 5-year empirical program searching for synthetic colorants from coal-tar aromatics — plausible given the known commercial value of dyes and the availability of aniline by the mid-1840s — could have reached the same result, since the team need only (1) obtain aniline fractions from coal tar, (2) systematically apply common oxidants, and (3) test residues on silk. The one-layer precursor rule is satisfied: aniline from coal tar is the single precursor, itself already available externally by ~1834–1840. No new scientific framework is required; empirical iteration over oxidants and substrates is sufficient. However, the discovery is flagged because the actual discovery was unambiguously accidental (Perkin was not searching for dyes), the productive impurity (toluidines in crude aniline) was not understood at the time, and a directed program without that accident might have missed this specific compound in favor of other less commercially viable chromogens. The earliest plausible date is therefore anchored to ~1845, when coal-tar aniline was reliably available in quantity and organic chemistry workshops were sufficiently equipped, with the gap from actual invention (1856) being modest and primarily reflecting the absence of a directed research intent rather than any missing technology or knowledge.

full_text:
Invention: Geissler tube
Year actually invented: 1857
Earliest plausible: 1852–1856
Earliest straightforward: 1854–1857
Confidence: High
Actual location: Bonn, Germany
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Germany, England, France
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The actual invention occurred in Bonn, Germany, which sits at the intersection of the strongest European glass-instrument-making tradition, the Ruhmkorff coil (Paris, 1851), and university-adjacent precision instrument workshops. England and France had comparable concentrations of glassblowers, induction coil access, and vacuum-pump expertise by the early 1850s.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Guericke piston vacuum pump [1650] — external/team-reachable
- Boyle/Hooke air pump refinements [1660s] — external/team-reachable
- Smeaton/Nairne improved air pumps [1750s–1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Geissler mercury-piston vacuum pump (the binding item) [1855, team-reachable by 1852–1854 with one-layer precursor work] — team-reachable
- Ruhmkorff induction coil (high-voltage AC source) [1851] — external/team-reachable
- Metal-to-glass hermetic sealing of electrodes (precision glassblowing, platinum wire seals) [extant by 1840s in scientific instrument trade] — external/team-reachable
- Sealed evacuated glass tube construction [extant by 1840s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Hauksbee's demonstration of glow discharge in partially evacuated glass globe [1709] — external/team-discoverable
- Morgan's observation of varied discharge colors at different pressures [1785] — external/team-discoverable
- Faraday's characterization of discharge striations and dark space in low-pressure tubes [1838] — external/team-discoverable
- Empirical knowledge that lower pressure (a few torr) produces stable, brilliant glow (not available from pre-Geissler pumps reaching only ~10–50 torr) — team-discoverable by iterative pump improvement
- No new scientific framework required; gas discharge at low pressure is empirically observable
Explanation: The binding limiting factor is Geissler's improved mercury-piston vacuum pump, which for the first time reliably evacuated tubes to pressures of roughly 1–10 torr — the range in which stable, brilliant glow discharge occurs. Earlier pumps (Guericke, Boyle, Smeaton) stalled at 10–50 torr, producing faint or unstable discharge that offered no compelling demonstration device. The Ruhmkorff induction coil (1851) supplied the necessary high-voltage AC and was commercially available almost immediately after its introduction; platinum-wire-to-glass sealing for hermetic electrodes was already within the skill set of European precision instrument workshops by the 1840s. A motivated inventor-artisan team possessing knowledge of Faraday's 1838 discharge observations and the Ruhmkorff coil could, within the rubric's one-layer allowance, develop a sufficiently improved mercury vacuum pump by approximately 1852–1854 and produce functional glow tubes by 1852–1856. No new scientific principle is required — only the mechanical engineering of a better pump and the glassblowing craft to seal metal electrodes, both achievable by the early 1850s given extant knowledge. The gap between earliest plausible and actual invention is only one to five years, reflecting that Geissler himself was both the instrument maker and the pump innovator, compressing what would otherwise require separate development tracks.

full_text:
Invention: Phonautograph
Year actually invented: 1857
Earliest plausible: 1800–1810
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1830
Confidence: High
Actual location: Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, United Kingdom, Germany
Geographic note: Paris was the center of acoustics research in the early 19th century (Biot, Savart, later Lissajous), making France the most likely origin. ALIGNED.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Smoked paper/glass substrate for tracing [ancient / pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Rotating cylinder or clock-driven drum mechanism [pre-1800, e.g., music box, kymograph precursors] — external/team-reachable
- Flexible membrane/diaphragm (e.g., animal bladder, thin metal sheet) [pre-1800] — external/team-reachable
- Rigid lightweight stylus or bristle attached to membrane [pre-1800, craft knowledge] — external/team-reachable
- Resonance/vibration analysis apparatus (Chladni figures, Savart wheel) [c. 1787–1820] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Sound as mechanical wave / vibration in air [Newton, 1687; well established by 1700] — external/team-discoverable
- Chladni's nodal figure experiments demonstrating vibrational modes [1787] — external/team-discoverable
- Savart's vibrating plate and membrane experiments [c. 1820s] — external/team-discoverable
- Stethoscope demonstrating membrane transmission of sound [Laennec, 1816] — external/team-discoverable
- Kymograph (Ludwig, 1847) as direct mechanical analog — team could independently reach this mechanism; not strictly required
Explanation: All physical components of the phonautograph — a thin diaphragm, a lightweight stylus, a smoked rotating cylinder — were within reach of a skilled artisan workshop by roughly 1800, and the conceptual framing (sound as vibration, vibration as traceable motion) was scientifically established no later than the late 18th century via Chladni. The binding constraint is not any single component but the synthetic idea of coupling a sound-driven diaphragm to a continuous recording substrate to capture a waveform over time, rather than merely observing static nodal patterns. Chladni figures (1787) show vibrational modes statically; the leap to a moving substrate producing a continuous time-domain trace is a conceptual and mechanical step a motivated team could plausibly reach by 1800–1810 without any new science. The slightly later "straightforward" window (1820–1830) reflects that Savart's membrane work and the stethoscope's popularization of the diaphragm concept would make the design more obvious, reducing iteration time. No serendipitous discovery or unavailable phenomenon is required; the gap between earliest plausible (~1800) and actual invention (1857) is roughly 50 years and is explained by the absence of a motivated patron framing the problem as waveform visualization for scientific analysis rather than sound reproduction.

full_text:
Invention: Lead-acid rechargeable battery
Year actually invented: 1859
Earliest plausible: 1836–1842
Earliest straightforward: 1840–1845
Confidence: High
Actual location: Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, United Kingdom, Germany, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Paris was a leading center of electrochemical research in the mid-19th century, and the UK and Germany had comparable infrastructure; the invention required no unique local resources.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Voltaic pile / galvanic cell [1800] — external (off-limits as discovery, usable after 1800)
- Dilute sulfuric acid production [pre-1800] — external
- Lead and lead compounds (metallurgical knowledge) [pre-1800] — external
- Rubber or gutta-percha as separator material [gutta-percha commercially available ~1843; rubber ~1830s] — external/team-reachable; cloth or wood spacers could substitute earlier
- Electrochemical cell construction (wire, vessel, terminals) [post-1800, well-established by 1820s] — external
- Faraday's electrolysis laws [1833] — external after 1833 (binding lower bound)

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Voltaic electricity drives electrolytic deposition [1800, Nicholson & Carlisle] — external after 1800
- Faraday's quantitative electrolysis laws [1833] — external after 1833; enables systematic charging protocols
- Lead forms compounds (litharge, minium, lead sulfate) empirically observable in acid [pre-1800 chemistry] — external
- Lead dioxide (PbO2) can be electrodeposited from lead plate in sulfuric acid — team-discoverable empirically by any group running charge cycles on lead electrodes
- Reversibility of the lead/lead-dioxide couple observable empirically upon discharge — team-discoverable with no new science

Explanation: The binding lower bound is Faraday's electrolysis laws (1833), which are external knowledge but essential for understanding that sustained current can convert lead plates into chemically distinct electrodes capable of storing charge. With the voltaic pile available after 1800 and Faraday's laws published by 1833, a motivated team of electrochemical engineers with access to lead sheet, dilute sulfuric acid, and a reliable galvanic current source could empirically discover the rechargeable lead-acid couple within five years of commencing systematic experiments on lead electrodes — placing the earliest plausible date around 1836–1842. The separator problem (preventing shorting of plates) is solvable with cloth or wood strips prior to commercial rubber availability, removing that as a hard constraint. No new scientific framework is required; the entire invention is accessible through empirical iteration on known materials and known electrochemical phenomena. The 17-year gap between the earliest plausible date (~1838) and actual invention (1859) reflects the historical reality that systematic investigation of rechargeable lead electrodes was not pursued until Planté's focused program, not any fundamental technological barrier.

full_text:
Invention: Carbon fiber (carbonized filament)
Year actually invented: 1860
Earliest plausible: 1820–1835
Earliest straightforward: 1835–1845
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Sunderland, England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Germany, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Britain led early electrical experimentation and had strong craft traditions in fine thread and kiln work; France and Germany had comparable chemical and artisan capabilities by the 1820s–1830s.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Charcoal production / pyrolysis of organic materials [antiquity] — external/team-reachable
- High-temperature sealed kilns or retorts (used in gas lighting / coke production) [~1790s] — external/team-reachable
- Fine cotton or linen thread spinning (industrial scale) [~1770s–1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Clay or iron sealed container fabrication [antiquity] — external/team-reachable
- Basic electrical conductivity measurement (galvanometer, simple battery) [~1820s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Lavoisier's combustion theory and role of oxygen in burning [1770s–1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Davy's arc lamp demonstrations showing carbon as electrical conductor [1802] — external/team-reachable
- Observation that charcoal conducts electricity [empirically demonstrable by ~1820] — external/team-discoverable
- Volta's pile enabling steady electrical current for experiments [1800] — external/team-reachable
- Gas retort practice demonstrating carbonization of organic matter in closed vessels [~1790s–1800s] — external/team-reachable
Explanation: The binding item is the combination of a motivating electrical application and the empirical realization that thin organic threads survive pyrolysis as intact flexible carbon conductors. The pyrolysis chemistry itself required no new science — charcoal-makers and gas-works engineers already carbonized organic matter in sealed retorts by the 1790s. The key intellectual leap is simply applying that process to pre-formed thin threads rather than bulk material, then recognizing the result as a useful electrical element. Davy's 1802 arc lamp demonstration established carbon's electrical conductance publicly, and Volta's pile (1800) made bench-scale electrical experiments routine. A motivated team working from ~1820 onward had all necessary materials, apparatus, and empirical precedents: fine cotton thread, sealed iron or clay retorts, a voltaic battery, and a galvanometer. The principal missing ingredient before ~1820 is a clear application motive — without incandescent lighting as a goal, the thin-filament form offers no obvious advantage over bulk charcoal. However, curiosity-driven electrical research or the search for fine carbon contacts for galvanic instruments could have provided sufficient motive by the early 1820s, placing the earliest plausible date at 1820–1835.

full_text:
Invention: Pasteurization
Year actually invented: 1864
Earliest plausible: 1780–1800
Earliest straightforward: 1820–1840
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Paris, France
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: France, United Kingdom, Netherlands, Italy
Geographic note: ALIGNED. France had strong wine and beer industries providing clear economic motivation, and Paris was a center of natural philosophy and empirical investigation; the technique could have emerged in any Western European nation with similar fermented-beverage industries and access to instrument makers.

Prerequisite technologies:
- Reliable thermometry (mercury-in-glass thermometer) [1714, Fahrenheit] — external/team-reachable
- Controlled heating vessels (water baths, chafing equipment) [pre-1700, culinary/apothecary tradition] — external/team-reachable
- Sealed glass bottles and corking technology [~1700s, wine industry] — external/team-reachable
- Spallanzani's boiling-broth experiments showing heat halts putrefaction [1768] — external/team-reachable
- Appert's canning / heat-preservation method [1809] — external/team-reachable (not required for earliest plausible window but accelerates discovery)

Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Observation that wine and beer spoil over time in ways that vary by temperature and exposure [longstanding empirical record] — external/team-discoverable
- Spallanzani's demonstration that controlled heat prevents putrefaction without requiring a theoretical explanation [1768] — external/team-discoverable
- Empirical observation that boiling destroys flavor, motivating search for lower effective temperatures [team-discoverable by iterative trial] — team-reachable
- Fahrenheit/Celsius temperature scales enabling reproducible experimental protocols [1714–1742] — external/team-reachable

Explanation: The binding item is reliable, graduated thermometry (available by ~1714–1742) combined with the economic motivation to preserve wine and beer quality. Spallanzani's 1768 work established empirically — without germ theory — that heat halts putrefaction; a motivated team working after ~1780 could systematically test sub-boiling temperature and time combinations on wine samples, observe spoilage outcomes over weeks, and converge on the 55–60°C window purely by trial and error. No new scientific framework is required: the team needs only the premise that heat does something to spoilage agents (already demonstrated by Spallanzani) and the thermometric tools to reproduce a temperature precisely. Germ theory accelerated understanding but is not necessary for the empirical discovery. The technique is therefore separable from Pasteur's theoretical framework, and the gap from ~1780 to 1864 reflects the absence of a sufficiently motivated, systematic experimental program rather than any missing prerequisite knowledge or technology.

actual_location: Sireuil, France (Martin's first commercial furnace); Germany (Siemens's regenerative furnace development)
possible_locations: United Kingdom; France; Germany; Belgium
geographic_note: ALIGNED. The process was developed in the most industrially advanced steelmaking nations of the era; all four possible locations had the required iron/steel industry, refractory materials supply, and skilled metallurgical workforce by the late 1850s.
full_text:
Invention: Siemens-Martin open-hearth steel process
Year actually invented: 1865
Earliest plausible: 1858–1865
Earliest straightforward: 1862–1867
Confidence: High
Actual location: Sireuil, France (Martin's first commercial furnace); Germany (Siemens's regenerative furnace development)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Germany, Belgium
Geographic note: ALIGNED. The process was developed in the most industrially advanced steelmaking nations of the era; all four possible locations had the required iron/steel industry, refractory materials supply, and skilled metallurgical workforce by the late 1850s.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Regenerative furnace (Siemens, 1857) [1857] — external; the binding enabler for reaching >1600°C in an open-hearth geometry
- Reverberatory (puddling) furnace design [c. 1784] — external; provides the open shallow-bath hearth geometry concept
- High-duty silica and magnesite refractory bricks [c. 1820s–1850s] — external; necessary to withstand sustained liquid-steel temperatures
- Coke-fired blast furnace and pig-iron supply chain [c. 1709, mature by 1800s] — external
- Bessemer converter (1856) — external; demonstrated that bulk liquid steel was commercially achievable, sharpened motivation
- Gas-producer / gasification technology for fuel delivery [c. 1850s] — external; regenerative furnace requires clean gaseous fuel
- Accurate thermometry and pyrometry (empirical practice) [mid-1800s] — team-reachable by iteration
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Heat recovery / regenerative principle (empirical thermodynamics) [1857, Siemens] — external; no new scientific framework required, purely engineering exploitation of known heat-exchange physics
- Iron-carbon phase behavior (empirical): that steel composition and properties depend on carbon content of the melt [known empirically by 1850s] — external
- Oxidation of carbon from pig iron in an open liquid bath (empirical steelmaking practice) [long known from puddling] — external
- Flame temperature achievable by preheating both air and gas feeds simultaneously (key empirical insight of regenerator) [1857] — external
Explanation: The binding item is Siemens's regenerative furnace of 1857. A motivated team working from 1858 onward could, within roughly five years, combine the regenerative furnace with an open reverberatory hearth: the engineering steps — scaling the regenerator to steelmaking size, selecting compatible refractories, empirically tuning pig-iron/scrap charge ratios, and controlling bath chemistry by sampling — all lie within one layer of precursor technology and require no new science. Without the regenerator, no alternative available before 1857 (pre-heated air blasts, conventional coal reverberatories) could reliably sustain the ~1600°C needed to keep a large steel bath fully molten and controllable; the puddling furnace operated well below that threshold and required solid/pasty iron manipulation. Martin's contribution was recognising that Siemens's furnace, already built for glass-melting, could be repurposed for steel if scrap and pig iron were charged together to balance carbon content — a metallurgical insight achievable by any skilled ironmaster with access to the regenerator. The 1858 floor is therefore set by the regenerator's availability; the 1865 ceiling reflects the time actually needed for empirical scale-up and commercial proof.

full_text:
Invention: Dynamite
Year actually invented: 1867
Earliest plausible: 1848–1852
Earliest straightforward: 1850–1855
Confidence: Flag (serendipitous accident in actual discovery; systematic testing would have found it)
Actual location: Krümmel, Germany (near Hamburg)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Germany, Sweden, United States
Geographic note: ALIGNED. Industrial chemistry and mining were active across Western Europe and the US by mid-century; any country with access to nitroglycerin synthesis and abundant porous mineral materials (kieselguhr, charcoal, sawdust) could have reached this combination. Nobel's German factory location reflects industrial infrastructure but confers no unique advantage over peers.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Nitroglycerin synthesis (Sobrero) [1847] — external
- Black powder / explosive blasting practice [pre-1800] — external
- Blasting cap / detonator concept [1830s–1840s] — external
- Industrial mining demand for safer high explosives [ongoing by 1840s] — external
- Kieselguhr (diatomite) as industrial insulating/packing material [well established by 1840s] — external
- Basic chemical absorption / filtration knowledge [pre-1800] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Instability and shock-sensitivity of pure nitroglycerin [1847, Sobrero] — external
- Empirical observation that porous solids absorb liquids and reduce spill/shock sensitivity [pre-1800, practical knowledge] — external/team-discoverable
- No new scientific framework required; purely empirical materials-testing against a known problem — team-reachable
Explanation: The binding item is nitroglycerin itself, available only after Sobrero's 1847 synthesis. Once nitroglycerin is in hand, the problem is immediately obvious — it is catastrophically shock-sensitive in liquid form — and the engineering solution (find a porous solid that immobilizes it) requires no scientific insight beyond practical chemistry. A motivated team in 1848 could have systematically tested charcoal, sawdust, clay, and mineral earths including kieselguhr as carriers within months; the five-year window gives ample time for iterative testing, stability trials, and field detonation tests. The discovery is flagged because Nobel's actual path was serendipitous (leakage into packing material), but systematic empirical testing — entirely within team capability — would have converged on kieselguhr or an equivalent absorbent by the early 1850s. No scientific theory beyond Sobrero's published synthesis and observable explosion behavior is required. The gap between earliest plausible (1848) and actual invention (1867) is roughly 19 years, explained primarily by the fact that Nobel and contemporaries initially pursued other stabilization strategies (emulsification, mixing with other liquids) and lacked the direct motivation that only came after the 1864 factory explosion.

geographic_note: MISMATCH. full_text:
Invention: Barbed wire
Year actually invented: 1867
Earliest plausible: 1800–1820
Earliest straightforward: 1850–1860
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: Ohio, USA; Illinois, USA
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: United Kingdom, France, Belgium, Germany, United States (northeast)
Geographic note: MISMATCH. The actual invention occurred in the American Midwest driven by prairie fencing demand, but the prerequisite wire-drawing and metalworking capacity existed in Western European industrial centers and the northeastern US well before the 1860s; the motivating context of vast unfenced land was geographically specific to the American frontier.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Wire drawing to consistent gauge (industrial scale) [~1300s, refined by 1600s] — external/team-reachable
- Spring steel and wrought iron wire production [~1600s, industrialized by 1780s] — external/team-reachable
- Wire twisting mechanisms [~1800s, well established by 1820] — external/team-reachable
- Basic metal-cutting and pointed-tip forming tools [ancient] — external/team-reachable
- Smooth wire fence patents and conceptual precedent [1850s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Tensile strength properties of drawn wire [empirically understood by 1700s] — external/team-discoverable
- That sharp points deter livestock more effectively than smooth barriers [empirical, observable] — external/team-discoverable
- Wire twisting increases structural integrity under lateral load [empirical, ~1800s] — external/team-discoverable
Explanation: The binding constraint on earliest plausible date is not materials science but demand and conceptual synthesis. Industrial-scale wire drawing capable of producing consistent-gauge iron wire existed in England and continental Europe by the early 1800s, and a motivated team with access to a wire-drawing workshop could empirically develop the barb-attachment and twisting mechanism within a five-year window without any new scientific knowledge. The earliest plausible window of 1800–1820 reflects the point at which all required wire-working infrastructure and tooling was reliably present in industrializing regions. The straightforward window of 1850–1860 reflects the period when smooth wire fencing was already being patented, making the conceptual leap to adding barbs trivially obvious; the actual 1867 date was delayed not by technical barriers but by the geographic and economic context — the explosive post-Civil War settlement of the American prairie created a uniquely large and immediate demand that made commercial development worthwhile, a demand that did not exist at scale in Europe where hedgerows and stone walls served fencing needs.

possible_locations: Germany; France; United Kingdom; Sweden
geographic_note: ALIGNED. Germany (Tübingen/Alsace) is in the possible-locations list. The discovery occurred within the German/French scientific sphere, which by 1840 held the leading organic chemistry infrastructure (Liebig school, Paris école de chimie); no geographic mismatch.
full_text:
Invention: PVC (polyvinyl chloride)
Year actually invented: 1872
Earliest plausible: 1840–1850
Earliest straightforward: 1850–1865
Confidence: Flag
Actual location: Germany (Tübingen / Alsace region)
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: Germany, France, United Kingdom, Sweden
Geographic note: ALIGNED — the actual discovery occurred in the German/French scientific sphere, which by 1840 had the leading organic chemistry infrastructure (Liebig school, Paris école); no mismatch between plausible and actual location.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Isolation/synthesis of ethylene (olefiant gas) [1795, Dutch chemists] — external/team-reachable
- Chlorine chemistry (Scheele) and handling of chlorine gas [1774] — external/team-reachable
- Synthesis of ethylene dichloride (Dutch oil of wine + Cl₂) [~1795] — external/team-reachable
- Regnault's synthesis of vinyl chloride from ethylene dichloride + alcoholic KOH [1835] — external/team-reachable
- Sealed glass vessel fabrication for handling volatile gases [well established by 1820s] — external/team-reachable
- Distillation and purification apparatus for volatile organochlorides [1820s–1830s] — external/team-reachable
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Scheele's discovery of chlorine [1774] — external/team-discoverable
- Gay-Lussac's characterization of chlorination reactions and HCl [1810s–1820s] — external/team-discoverable
- Regnault's organic chlorine series including vinyl chloride synthesis [1835] — external/team-reachable (published)
- Berzelius/Liebig-era empirical observation that certain organic substances "thicken" or solidify on prolonged light exposure [1830s–1840s] — external/team-discoverable
- No understanding of free-radical polymerization (Gomberg 1900) required — the phenomenon is observable as an empirical curiosity without mechanistic explanation
- Sunlight as a chemical initiator recognized empirically (photochemical darkening of silver salts, etc.) [1820s–1830s] — external/team-reachable
Explanation: The binding item is Regnault's 1835 published synthesis of vinyl chloride, which provides the direct precursor. A motivated team operating in a well-equipped organic chemistry laboratory — as existed in Germany or France by the early 1840s — could reproduce Regnault's synthesis, seal purified vinyl chloride in glass tubes, and expose them to direct sunlight as part of a systematic study of how chlorinated organic gases behave under various conditions. The photo-initiated polymerization is flagged as serendipitous in the strict rubric sense: no rational engineering program in 1840 would have targeted UV-initiated chain-growth polymerization as a goal, because the concept did not exist. However, the observation itself requires only that a tube of vinyl chloride sit in a sunny window, an entirely plausible experimental accident in an active research setting. The team-reachable path is therefore: (1) reproduce Regnault's vinyl chloride, (2) incidentally expose to sunlight, (3) observe and characterize the white solid. This places the earliest plausible date at ~1840 (immediately after Regnault's work became widely known), with "straightforward" meaning a program deliberately testing photochemical effects on newly synthesized organochlorides, which was not uncommon practice in the 1850s–1865 German school. The Flag reflects that the initiating step is an accidental light exposure rather than a directed synthesis goal; a purely directed program without the serendipitous trigger would likely never have produced PVC in this period.

full_text:
Invention: Early stainless steel (chromium steel)
Year actually invented: 1872
Earliest plausible: 1825–1835
Earliest straightforward: 1835–1845
Confidence: Medium
Actual location: England
Possible locations at earliest plausible date: England, France, Sweden, Germany
Geographic note: England is ALIGNED — Britain led industrial steel production and had established metalworking workshops with access to chromium compounds and ferrochromium research originating from France (Berthier, 1821). France is equally plausible as the origin of ferrochromium work.
Prerequisite technologies:
- Iron/steel production (crucible steel, cementation) [pre-1800] — external
- Chromium isolation (Vauquelin) [1798] — external
- Ferrochromium production (Berthier) [1821] — external
- Corrosion testing methods (acid immersion, outdoor exposure) [pre-1800] — external
- Crucible alloying technique for adding metals to molten steel [~1750] — external
Scientific theories / key empirical observations:
- Chromium compounds studied for properties (Davy and others) [early 1800s] — external
- Berthier's observation that chromium addition improves hardness in iron [1821] — external
- Empirical observation that chromium forms stable oxides resistant to acid [~1800–1820] — team-discoverable
- No theoretical understanding of passivation required — corrosion resistance discoverable by systematic immersion/acid testing — team-discoverable
Explanation: By 1821, all binding prerequisites are in place: chromium had been isolated (1798), ferrochromium had been produced and its hardening effect noted by Berthier (1821), and crucible steel alloying was routine industrial practice in Britain, France, Sweden, and Germany. A motivated team could obtain ferrochromium or chromium oxide, systematically vary chromium additions to crucible steel across a range of compositions, and measure corrosion resistance by acid immersion — a standard empirical test requiring no new science. The binding item is Berthier's 1821 ferrochromium work, which provides both the practical precursor material and the empirical motivation to explore chromium-steel alloys further. A five-year campaign beginning ~1822–1825 could plausibly yield a patentable chromium steel with noted corrosion resistance by 1825–1835; the process becomes straightforward once commercial ferrochromium supply and systematic alloy testing are routine, placing the straightforward window at 1835–1845. The 37-year gap to actual invention (1872) reflects lack of industrial motivation and awareness, not technical impossibility.

possible_locations: England; Belgium; France; Germany; United States
